ce-fn.name: ce-fn
ce-fn.currentState: ce-fn is an app-tier Rust crate (edition 2024, ~1740 LOC across 6 source files) that composes existing CE primitives via the ce-rs SDK and ce-cap into a Cloud-Functions/Cloud-Run-style control plane. The architecture is clean and idiomatic: `function.rs` (Function/Handler/Deployment specs + JSON Registry), `placement.rs` (pure atlas-filter + reputation-rank host selection), `protocol.rs` (InvokeRequest/InvokeResponse/TriggerEvent wire types over hex+JSON), `caps.rs` (ce-cap grant/authorize wrappers), `client.rs` (FnClient: deploy/invoke/kill/run_trigger + a SeenWindow dedup), and a clap CLI in `src/bin/ce-fn.rs`. What genuinely works AND is testable offline: name validation, registry persistence, pure placement/ranking, protocol round-trips, and capability grant/authorize/revoke. All the SDK methods it calls (mesh_deploy, mesh_deploy_wasm, request, subscribe, messages, atlas, history.delivered_work, mesh_kill) exist in ce-rs. The decisive gap is that there is NO serve-side runtime anywhere in the workspace that answers `ce-fn/invoke` AppRequests — the README itself admits this ("That serve-side daemon is the sibling deliverable... ce-fn ships the client/control plane"). Consequently `invoke` and `on`/triggers cannot complete end-to-end against any real host (they would block until the 600s timeout). There are no todo!()/unimplemented!()/panic! in non-test paths and no unwrap() in production code — all unwrap/expect occur only in `#[cfg(test)]` and tests/. This is a well-written half (control plane only) of a two-half product.
plan: 1. Build the serve-side runtime (ce-fn-serve daemon, mirroring rdev serve): subscribe to ce-fn/invoke, on each AppRequest decode InvokeRequest, enforce caps via caps::authorize_invoke, run the named handler (exec into the already-deployed container cell or instantiate the WASM module), capture stdout/exit, and reply with InvokeResponse via CeClient::reply. Without this nothing in the crate works end-to-end. Ship it with at least one container and one WASM example handler.

2. Add an end-to-end integration test (gated, --ignored) that starts a local node + the runtime, deploys a trivial echo function, invokes it, asserts the round-trip, and kills it. This is the only test that exercises the product's reason for existing.

3. Make the trigger loop reliable: switch run_trigger from polling CeClient::messages to the SSE messages_stream, keep the SeenWindow dedup, and add bounded retry with backoff per invocation. Document at-least-once vs at-most-once and add a failure-path test for dropped/duplicate/malformed events.

4. Add env vars and secrets to Function (env: Vec<(String,String)>, secrets referencing a store), thread them through BidSpec/mesh_deploy and into the runtime's handler launch. This is table-stakes FaaS and currently entirely absent.

5. Introduce per-invocation billing or a payment-channel path: open a channel on deploy, sign a receipt per invoke in invoke_with, so the economics match FaaS pay-per-call rather than a held cell. At minimum document and expose invocation accounting.

6. Add `ce-fn logs` and basic observability: stream handler stdout/stderr, and surface invocation count/latency/error metrics (the runtime publishes them on a metrics topic or the registry records last-invoked/error counts).

7. Harden inputs and limits: cap InvokeRequest/InvokeResponse payload size (constant + validation on encode and decode), validate module_hash and host node-id as 64-hex in the deploy path, and reject oversized hex on decode to close the OOM/DoS vector.

8. Make placement resilient: rank top-N (not just best), and on deploy iterate candidates with retry/fallback when a host fails (handle the documented atlas TOCTOU). Parallelize the history fan-out with a timeout and stop treating fetch errors as zero reputation silently.

9. Make the Registry robust: atomic temp-file+rename writes, an advisory file lock against concurrent CLI processes, a schema version field with migration, and a `ce-fn forget <name> --force` to drop entries whose host is unreachable. Add a reconcile that checks actual host job state.
10. Add function versioning: key the registry by name+revision, support deploy creating a new revision, `ce-fn rollback`, and (optionally) traffic-split across revisions for canary.

11. Add richer trigger types: an HTTP-ingress trigger (public invoke URL via the node), a cron/scheduler trigger, and a structured CloudEvents mapping for TriggerEvent so storage/pubsub producers integrate cleanly.

12. Add concurrency/scale controls to Function (replicas, min/max instances, max-concurrency) and place multiple cells across the top-N hosts with client-side load balancing on invoke; expose scale-to-zero vs warm.

13. Fill test and doc gaps: property tests for protocol and placement, a normative serve protocol spec doc, an examples/ directory with runnable handlers, an architecture/lifecycle doc, and a clear 'data plane not included / Cloud-Run-style billing' banner at the top of the README.

featureGaps.notes: No daemon answers ce-fn/invoke AppRequests by running the deployed container/WASM and replying with InvokeResponse. Without it deploy places a cell but invoke/triggers never return output. This is THE blocker — the analog of GCF actually running your code.

GCF has min/max instances, max-concurrent-requests-per-instance, and scale-to-zero. ce-fn deploys exactly one cell on one host (deploy_on picks a single Candidate) with no replica count, no per-host concurrency cap, no horizontal scale-out, no scale-to-zero. No load distribution across multiple hosts for the same function.

No warm-pool, no pre-pull, no keep-alive vs scale-to-zero distinction. The 600s default invoke timeout is a blunt substitute. No cold-start metric or mitigation; a fresh container pull blocks the caller with no separate cold-start budget.

invoke does a single request() with no retry/backoff. Triggers are explicitly at-most-once (best-effort), the opposite of GCF event triggers which are at-least-once with retry policy and dead-letter. No retry config, no DLQ, no exponential backoff.

README states 'no separate meter' — billing is the deploy-time job bid (GB-seconds of the held cell), not per-invocation. GCF bills per invocation + compute time + egress. invoke() does not debit credits at all; a function holds a cell via heartbeats regardless of call count. The economic model is Cloud-Run-min-instance, not FaaS pay-per-call.

Function has no env field and no secrets. BidSpec is passed image/cmd/cpu/mem/duration/bid only. GCF supports --set-env-vars and Secret Manager mounts. No way to inject config or credentials into a handler.

No `ce-fn logs` command, no log streaming, no metrics (invocation count, latency, error rate), no tracing. CLI uses println! for status. README lists logs as a FaaS feature but none is implemented. InvokeResponse carries only output_hex/error, no stdout/stderr separation surfaced.

featureGaps.feature: HTTP/event trigger types beyond raw pubsub
featureGaps.notes: Only a single pubsub-topic trigger (`on`). GCF has HTTP triggers (public URL), Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, Firestore, scheduler/cron, and Eventarc. No HTTP ingress, no cron trigger, no structured CloudEvents mapping (TriggerEvent is opaque hex).
Registry keys by name only; deploy replaces in place (insert overwrites). No revisions, no gradual rollout / traffic splitting, no rollback to a prior version, no aliases.

featureGaps.feature: Concurrency-aware request limits / payload size limits
featureGaps.notes: No max payload size on InvokeRequest (GCF caps request/response at 32MB for HTTP, 10MB for event). No per-function rate limiting or quota.

featureGaps.feature: Function update without redeploy / health checks
featureGaps.notes: No update-in-place of env/resources, no readiness/liveness probe, no graceful drain on kill.
`ls` shows static spec; no `describe`/`status` showing whether the cell is actually alive on the host, last-invoked, or job state. No reconciliation between registry and actual host job state.

robustnessIssues.issue: Trigger loop polls a bounded best-effort inbox ring (CeClient::messages) every 250ms; under load or any GC of the ring, events are silently dropped before the dedup window even sees them. messages_stream (SSE) exists in ce-rs and would be reliable, but run_trigger ignores it. Lost events = lost invocations with no record.

InvokeRequest payload has no size limit. A caller can submit an arbitrarily large payload; it is hex-encoded (2x blowup) into a single AppRequest and held entirely in memory on both ends. No bound on payload_hex length on decode either → DoS / OOM vector.

deploy() selects exactly one host and on failure has no fallback to the next-ranked candidate; if mesh_deploy errors (host went away between atlas read and deploy — a known TOCTOU), the whole deploy fails rather than retrying the next Candidate. select_host even comments on the atlas-changed race but does not handle it at deploy time.

kill() removes the function from the registry only after a successful mesh_kill, but if mesh_kill succeeds and remove() returns None (race), it errors 'vanished from registry' — and conversely a failed mesh_kill leaves a stale registry entry pointing at a dead/leaked cell with no reconciliation path. No way to force-forget a deployment whose host is unreachable.

Registry is a single JSON file written non-atomically (std::fs::write truncates then writes); a crash or two concurrent CLI processes mid-write corrupts it. No file lock, no temp-file+rename, no schema version field for migration.

WASM module_hash and host node-id strings are not validated as 64-hex anywhere in the deploy path; only the function name is validated. A malformed module_hash / host id is passed straight to the SDK, surfacing as an opaque downstream error. parse_node_id validates only for the grant audience.

select_host does an O(pool) sequential history() fan-out with per-host .unwrap_or(0) that silently treats any history fetch error as zero reputation — a transient API error on the strongest host's history could derank it. Also no timeout/parallelism; a slow node stalls placement.

encode() methods swallow serde errors via unwrap_or_default(), producing empty bytes on a (practically impossible but unhandled) serialization failure rather than surfacing an error; a downstream decode then fails with a confusing 'malformed' message.

Grant CLI uses default nonce=1 and Resource::Any with no warning; two grants minted with default args share a nonce (revocation collision) and Any-scoped tokens are broader than most users expect. No uniqueness check or guidance enforced.

Invoke with no cap and a host that requires authorization will fail remotely, but the client never sets a default/derived cap and the error path is entirely dependent on the (nonexistent) runtime — there is no client-side preflight that the caller is authorized.

testGaps.area: End-to-end invoke against a real or mock runtime (no serve-side exists, so the core path is wholly untested)
FnClient::deploy / deploy_on / invoke / kill / run_trigger — all network-touching client methods have zero tests (no mock CeClient; CeClient is concrete, not a trait, so it cannot be stubbed)
Trigger loop: dedup-under-replay, dropped-message handling, shutdown mid-event, malformed event payloads
Placement TOCTOU and deploy fallback to next candidate when chosen host fails
Registry concurrent/atomic write, corruption recovery, schema migration
Property tests for protocol round-trips (arbitrary bytes/payloads, large payloads, invalid hex) and placement ranking invariants (total order, stability)
Capability enforcement at the invoke boundary end-to-end (authorize_invoke is unit-tested in isolation but never wired to an invoke call)
CLI argument parsing / build_handler edge cases are only partially covered (mutually-exclusive flags, stdin payload, --select repetition) — no CLI integration test harness

docGaps: No serve-side runtime spec: the wire contract (ce-fn/invoke request/response semantics, error codes, capability enforcement point) is described in prose but there is no normative protocol doc or reference implementation for whoever builds the host daemon.
No examples/ directory and no runnable doctest (the lib.rs example is `no_run`). A FaaS product needs at least one end-to-end example handler (container + WASM) and a sample trigger producer.

README 'Status & limits' honestly admits the runtime is missing, but the top-of-README and Cargo description sell it as a working 'deploy/invoke/trigger' product — the missing data plane should be stated up front, not buried.

No architecture/sequence doc for the deploy→escrow→heartbeat→settle lifecycle, nor how invoke billing relates (or does not) to the held cell — the billing section contradicts a per-invocation FaaS mental model and needs an explicit 'this is Cloud-Run-style, not pay-per-call' callout.
No rustdoc on several public protocol fields' invariants (e.g. exit_code semantics, content_type usage by the runtime) and no documented size/limit constants.
No CONTRIBUTING/testing doc explaining that all tests are offline and what a real integration test would require (a running node + a runtime).

ce-gke.name: ce-gke
ce-gke.currentState: ce-gke is a single Rust crate (lib + `ce-gke` CLI binary, ~3270 LOC, edition 2024, one commit) implementing a GKE-style declarative orchestrator entirely as a client over CE primitives — no control plane. The architecture is genuinely good and cleanly layered: pure planners (spec/placement/reconcile/rollout) are separated from a side-effecting `MeshDriver` trait with a real `CeDriver` (over the ce-rs SDK's mesh_deploy/mesh_kill/atlas/job) and a deterministic `FakeDriver` for failure injection. The `Controller::tick`/`converge` loop wires phase-refresh, rolling-update step planning, kill-with-retry, and atlas-ranked placement with host fallback and spread penalty. What genuinely works: manifest parse (YAML/JSON), content-addressed revision hashing, spec validation, capacity/tag/freshness-based placement scoring, steady-state reconcile (scale up/down, reap+replace failures), rolling-update and recreate step planning honoring surge/unavailable budgets, ce-cap grant issue + local preflight, atomic JSON state persistence, and a kubectl-shaped CLI (apply/get/scale/rollout/delete). It is well-tested (~105 unit + property tests, all on real logic). What is stubbed/absent: there is no long-running daemon — reconciliation only happens during a CLI command's bounded tick budget, so self-healing stops the moment the process exits. There are no Services/networking, no health probes (liveness/readiness are inferred only from CE job status, not HTTP/exec checks), no namespaces, no ConfigMaps/Secrets/volumes, and no autoscaling. The `phase()` call only queries the local payer node's job record, not the remote host, so a host that silently drops a job is only caught heuristically.
plan: Add a long-running `ce-gke run`/`watch` daemon command that loops reconcile on a configurable interval indefinitely (with graceful SIGINT shutdown via tokio), reconciling all managed deployments — this turns the claimed 'self-healing' into reality and is the single highest-leverage gap vs GKE.
Fix the `truncate` UTF-8 panic: slice on a char boundary (use char_indices or chars().take) and add image-name charset/length validation in Deployment::validate; add a multibyte-input unit test.

Implement health probes: add liveness/readiness probe fields to the spec (http/tcp/exec + interval/threshold), make the driver actually probe the remote replica (over mesh exec or an HTTP endpoint), and feed real readiness into reconcile/rollout instead of inferring solely from CE job status.

Make `phase()` query the actual host/remote job rather than only the local payer's record, and replace controller.refresh's blanket Err->Failed with bounded retry + per-replica failure counting so a transient local-API blip cannot trigger a fleet-wide reschedule storm.

Add a Service abstraction backed by the ce-rs advertise_service/find_service DHT primitives: register the healthy replica set as a named service, expose endpoint discovery, and document client-side load balancing.

Wire auth::preflight into apply/scale/rollout so a bad/expired/over-broad grant fails fast with a clear capability error before any deploy is attempted; add balance/affordability pre-check (replicas*bid vs node balance) and a sane upper bound on replicas.
Add a file lock (or lock file) around state load+save in main.rs to prevent the lost-update race between concurrent ce-gke processes; add a concurrency test.
Add namespaces: scope Store keys by (namespace,name), add a --namespace flag, and lay groundwork for per-namespace quota.
Add rollout history + `rollout undo`/`pause`/`resume`: keep the last N specs in Store, allow rolling back to a prior revision and pausing a roll mid-flight.

Improve placement: ingest real per-job resource accounting if the atlas can expose it, add hard anti-affinity / topology spread keys to the spec, and add a property test asserting placement never over-commits a host's true capacity.
Add env vars / ConfigMap / Secret injection and volume support to the Deployment spec and the BidSpec mapping in the driver, so real workloads can be configured.

Add CLI/integration tests that drive the actual apply/scale/rollout/delete/get handlers against FakeDriver, plus mock-HTTP tests for CeDriver error mapping (402, timeout); enforce the README's test/clippy claims in CI.
Add a horizontal autoscaler that scales replicas on observed load/metrics from the atlas, with min/max bounds and cooldown.

Document the architecture (ADR on CLI-vs-daemon), version the gke-state.json format with a migration path, add a CHANGELOG, and expand examples/ (recreate strategy, JSON manifest, grant workflow as a runnable doctest).

featureGaps.notes: GKE's controllers reconcile forever. Here reconciliation only runs inside a CLI command's bounded `max_ticks` loop (main.rs reconcile_to_convergence). After the process exits nothing watches the fleet, so 'self-healing' is a misnomer — a replica that fails after `ce-gke apply` returns is never replaced until the operator manually re-runs `rollout`. Need a `ce-gke run`/`watch` daemon that loops reconcile on an interval indefinitely.

No liveness/readiness probe concept (HTTP GET, TCP, exec). Phase is derived solely from CE job status strings (reconcile.rs Phase::from_job_status). A container that is 'running' per the host but serving 500s or deadlocked is counted Running and never restarted. Real GKE distinguishes ready (gates traffic) from live (triggers restart).

No Service abstraction, no stable virtual IP/DNS, no endpoint set, no load balancing across replicas. ce-rs already exposes advertise_service/find_service (DHT service discovery) which ce-gke does not use at all. Replicas are placed but unreachable as a unit; nothing routes traffic to the healthy set.
Replica count is static; no metric-driven scaling. GKE HPA scales on CPU/custom metrics. The atlas exposes running_jobs/capacity that could feed a simple autoscaler.
Store keys deployments by bare name in one flat BTreeMap (state.rs). No namespace scoping, so two operators/teams collide on names; no per-namespace quota.
Deployment spec carries only image/command/cpu/mem/duration/bid. No env vars, no ConfigMap/Secret mounting, no volumes/persistent storage. Real workloads need configuration and state.
No revision history beyond the current spec; cannot `rollout undo` to a prior image or pause a roll mid-flight. Store keeps only the latest spec (state.rs upsert overwrites).

featureGaps.feature: Pod disruption budgets / anti-affinity / topology spread
featureGaps.notes: Placement spreads via a -150 score penalty per chosen host (controller.rs) but there is no hard anti-affinity, no zone/rack topology spread, no PodDisruptionBudget protecting availability during voluntary host drain.

featureGaps.feature: Resource quotas / limits enforcement & requests vs limits
featureGaps.notes: Single resource value per replica (request==limit). No namespace ResourceQuota, no LimitRange, no burstable QoS classes.

featureGaps.feature: Observability: events, conditions, structured status, logs
featureGaps.notes: Status is a printed READY count via println! in get(). No structured Deployment conditions (Available/Progressing), no event stream, no `ce-gke logs`, no metrics export.
Only the Deployment kind exists. No batch Job, CronJob, StatefulSet (stable identity/ordered), or DaemonSet (one-per-host) workload types.

robustnessIssues.issue: UTF-8 slicing panic in `truncate`: `&s[..n.saturating_sub(3)]` slices on a byte index that can fall inside a multibyte UTF-8 char. `truncate(&m.spec.image, 24)` is called on the image string, and image is only validated as non-empty (spec.rs validate) — not restricted to ASCII — so an image name with multibyte chars longer than 24 bytes will panic the `get` command.
robustnessIssues.location: src/main.rs:284-290 (truncate), called at main.rs:271-272; image charset unchecked in src/spec.rs:124-127

robustnessIssues.issue: No daemon means self-healing is only as live as a manually-run command; between CLI invocations failures are not detected or repaired. This is a correctness/availability gap, not just a feature gap — the README and crate description claim 'self-healing loop' but the loop terminates with the process.
robustnessIssues.location: src/main.rs:179-229 (reconcile_to_convergence bounded by max_ticks); claim in src/lib.rs:8 and README

robustnessIssues.issue: `phase()` in CeDriver ignores node_id and only reads the local node's job record (self.ce.job(job_id)). A replica deployed on a remote host whose status the local payer no longer tracks (or which the host failed to report) is invisible; remote liveness is not actually probed. Combined with the fail-safe Err->Failed in controller.refresh, a transient local API hiccup can mass-mark all replicas Failed and trigger a thundering reschedule.
robustnessIssues.location: src/driver.rs:96-101 (phase), src/controller.rs:62-70 (refresh maps any Err to Failed)

robustnessIssues.issue: Placement free-capacity heuristic charges every running job one replica-of-this-deployment worth of CPU/mem (free_capacity, placement.rs). This is a coarse guess; on a shared host running heterogeneous jobs it can both over- and under-estimate, leading to over-commit (placing where there is no real room) or starvation (skipping a host with plenty of room for small jobs). No real per-job resource accounting from the atlas.

No bid/credit balance pre-check before deploy. The CLI funds each replica with `bid` (locked) but never validates the operator has balance for replicas*bid; a large `replicas` value silently attempts many deploys that the node will reject with 402, surfacing only as place_failures. No DoS bound on replica count either (u32 up to ~4.3B accepted by validate).
robustnessIssues.location: src/spec.rs:128-147 (validate accepts any replicas; no upper bound), src/controller.rs:151-196

robustnessIssues.issue: `max_attempts` placement cap uses `step.to_place.saturating_mul(4).max(candidates.len()+1)`; with many candidates all transiently failing, attempts can be large but bounded — acceptable, but there is no backoff between deploy attempts within a tick, so a flapping host gets hammered in a tight loop.

Concurrency: ce-gke assumes a single operator process. Two concurrent `ce-gke` invocations on the same state file race — last writer wins on the atomic rename (state.rs save), silently dropping the other's replica handles. No file lock.
`FakeDriver` uses `.expect("lock")` on a std::sync::Mutex throughout. This is test-only code so it is acceptable per standards, but a poisoned lock from a panicking test would cascade.

Grant token is forwarded but never preflight-verified by the CLI before hitting the mesh, despite auth::preflight existing for exactly this. A wrong/expired token only fails per-deploy as an opaque place_failure rather than failing fast with a clear capability error.
robustnessIssues.location: src/main.rs (apply/scale never call auth::preflight); capability in src/auth.rs:54-67 unused by binary

testGaps.area: Long-running daemon reconcile loop (does not exist; once added, needs interval/cancellation tests)

CLI command handlers (apply/scale/rollout/delete/get) end-to-end against FakeDriver — currently only helper fns (short/truncate/fail_note/read_manifest) are tested, the actual command logic in main.rs is untested
truncate/short with multibyte UTF-8 input (the panic path) — no test exercises non-ASCII image names
CeDriver real HTTP paths (mesh_deploy/mesh_kill/atlas/job error mapping, 402 no-balance, network timeout) — no mock-HTTP integration test; all driver tests use FakeDriver
Concurrent state-file access / lost-update race between two ce-gke processes
refresh() mass-Failed behavior when the node API transiently errors (every Err->Failed) — no test asserts the reschedule-storm risk
Property test for reconcile: count-conservation holds but no property asserting placement never over-commits a host's real capacity, nor that spread is maximized
State migration / forward-compat of gke-state.json across schema changes (new spec fields) — only same-version roundtrip is tested

docGaps: README claims '~105 tests, all green' and 'cargo clippy clean' — unverified assertions that should be CI-enforced, not prose; no CI workflow content reviewed beyond .github existing.

No architecture/ADR doc explaining the deliberate 'no daemon, CLI-driven reconcile' tradeoff vs GKE's always-on controllers; this surprising limitation is buried and contradicts the 'self-healing loop' framing in lib.rs:8 and README.
No CHANGELOG, no versioning/stability notes for the gke-state.json on-disk format (it is a public-ish contract across invocations).
docs/ directory does not exist for this crate; the parent PLAN/12-google-infra-portfolio.md is referenced but not present alongside the crate.

Rustdoc is strong on modules but the binary's command semantics (what convergence means, what 'not fully converged' implies operationally, how to recover) live only in main.rs help text; an examples/ dir has only one web.yaml and no JSON manifest example, no recreate-strategy example, no grant workflow example.

No doctest is actually compiled-and-run for the auth/grant workflow shown in README (it is a plain ```rust block with `#` lines but uses undefined identifiers host_identity/orchestrator_node_id, so it would not run as a doctest if enabled).

ce-query.name: ce-query
ce-query.currentState: ce-query is a BigQuery-shaped distributed map-reduce engine over CE content-addressed blob datasets, built as an app on the ce-rs SDK (no node changes). The pure core is genuinely strong and real, not stubbed: NDJSON dataset sharding (dataset.rs), a filter-aggregate-group query model with a pure deterministic map step (query.rs), a monoid combiner algebra for the reduce with associativity/commutativity/identity (combine.rs), rendezvous-hash (HRW) shard→host placement with deterministic failover ranking (plan.rs), a distributed executor with retry/redistribute-on-drop over a MapHost trait seam (engine.rs), two real MapHost impls — coordinator-local fetch and true mesh request/reply — plus an on-host serve_map agent (mesh.rs), a hand-written SQL-ish parser covering SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BY/ORDER BY/LIMIT (sql.rs), an in-memory hash-join + co-partitioned distributed_join (join.rs), ce-cap dataset-scoped query:read tokens (caps.rs), and an atomic JSON catalog (catalog.rs). Production paths are clean of unwrap/expect/panic/todo (the only non-test .expect is a provably-safe key lookup in main.rs:175). Testing is unusually good for this stage: ~109 unit tests plus proptests proving the algebra laws, shard-count invariance, JSON round-trips, HRW balance/re-homing, order/limit, join cardinality, and engine failure-injection (dead hosts, transient 5xx, missing blobs, attempt caps). The gaps vs real BigQuery are scope, not soundness: no columnar/predicate-pushdown/projection, joins are not wired into SQL or the distributed engine (run only in one process), the engine fans shards sequentially (await per shard) rather than concurrently, there is no redundant/quorum execution for result verification, and revocation is hardcoded off in the serve agent.
plan: Make engine::run fan shards concurrently: replace the sequential for-loop (engine.rs:125) with a bounded FuturesUnordered/JoinSet (configurable max in-flight), preserving the per-shard retry/redistribute walk; add an overall query deadline and a per-attempt timeout. Add concurrency tests asserting out-of-order partial arrival still reduces correctly.

Add redundant/quorum execution for result verification: optionally map each shard on K hosts (K from RunConfig), compare the K Partials, and require agreement (or majority); surface a HostDisagreement error. Test with a mock host that returns a corrupted Partial — this is the missing 'verification via redundancy' BigQuery analog and the strongest trust win on an open mesh.

Fix numeric correctness: add integer SUM/COUNT accumulators (i128 base units per the money convention) and use a deterministic summation (e.g. Kahan or sorted/pairwise) for f64 AVG/SUM; extend the associativity/commutativity proptests to fractional and large-magnitude inputs so they actually exercise the float path.

Support projection (non-aggregate) queries: relax Query::validate (query.rs:275), add a Project/columns path to map_shard and the SQL parser (`SELECT a, b FROM t WHERE ...`), with LIMIT and a result-row cap. This unlocks the single most common BigQuery query shape.

Add predicate/projection pushdown to the shard scan: have serve_map/LocalMapHost apply the WHERE predicate and project only needed columns while decoding (mesh.rs), and add per-shard min/max column stats to Shard so the planner can skip shards that cannot match (partition/skip pruning). This is BigQuery's core efficiency story.

Add query cost limits: estimate bytes/rows to scan from Dataset/Shard counts, enforce a max-bytes/max-rows/max-groups budget in engine::run, return a clear LimitExceeded error before dispatch, and wire per-query credit pricing through the CE economy. Prevents unbounded memory and spend (DoS).

Wire JOIN through SQL and the distributed engine: extend sql.rs to parse `JOIN ... ON`, and make engine::run execute co-partitioned joins across hosts (each host owns matching left/right buckets) feeding into the aggregate map — turning join.rs from in-process reference semantics into a real distributed operator.

Harden the serve agent: consult on-chain revocation (replace `|_,_| false` at main.rs:338 with a real revocation lookup), load accepted_roots and self_tags from config, spawn each map request concurrently to remove head-of-line blocking, and cap inbound request size/rate.

Replace string-substring error classification (mesh.rs:148-169) with typed SDK errors from ce-rs (or a structured error enum the SDK exposes), so MissingBlob/Timeout/HostError are determined reliably.

Add a catalog write lock (advisory file lock or atomic compare-and-swap on a version field) so concurrent `dataset add` cannot clobber, plus dataset append (add shards to an existing dataset) and a dataset version/generation field for time-travel.
Add streaming/incremental reduce (fold partials as they arrive rather than buffering a Vec) and pagination for large group sets; add memory-bounds tests for high-cardinality GROUP BY.

Documentation and examples: ship examples/ with a sample sales.ndjson and a runnable quickstart, fix README doctests to compile, add an honest 'vs BigQuery' limitations matrix, and document the f64 precision and revocation caveats.

featureGaps.notes: engine::run (engine.rs:125) processes shards in a sequential `for task ... { host.map(...).await }` loop, so the headline 'fan map tasks across hosts' is actually serialized — wall-clock scales with shard count, not host count. Needs FuturesUnordered/JoinSet with a bounded concurrency limit.

Shards are whole-row NDJSON fetched and fully decoded on every query (mesh.rs serve_map/LocalMapHost). No column pruning, no partition pruning, no skip-on-stats. BigQuery's core value (scan only needed columns/partitions) is absent; the whole shard is always materialized.
Query::validate (query.rs:275) rejects any query with zero aggregates: `SELECT a, b FROM t WHERE ...` (non-aggregate projection) is unsupported. BigQuery's most common query shape cannot run.

featureGaps.feature: JOIN in SQL and across the mesh
featureGaps.notes: join.rs hash_join/distributed_join exist and are tested, but are never reachable from sql::parse (explicitly out of scope) nor from engine::run — distributed_join still executes in one process over fully-materialized row vecs, so cross-dataset joins do not actually distribute or stream.

plan.rs assigns each shard to exactly one primary host; on success the result is trusted. There is no N-way redundant map + agreement/quorum check, so a single malicious or buggy host silently corrupts an aggregate. This is the explicit 'result verification via redundancy' reference feature and is missing.

No bytes-scanned estimate, no max-bytes-billed guard, no row/shard/time budget, no per-query credit pricing via the CE economy. RunReport tracks attempts only. A query over a huge dataset has no cost ceiling (DoS / unbounded spend).
sql.rs supports only COUNT(*)/SUM/MIN/MAX/AVG of a bare column, comparison-only WHERE, no arithmetic/functions in SELECT or WHERE, no HAVING, no DISTINCT, no nested queries.

All aggregates coerce to f64 (query.rs as_f64, Accum::Sum(f64)). SUM over large counts or money loses precision and is non-deterministic across reorderings at the ULP level — violates the project's integer-base-unit money convention. No integer SUM or decimal type.

Partials are collected into a Vec then reduced once (engine.rs:168); results and shards are fully buffered in memory. No streaming reduce-as-arrive (despite the monoid supporting it) and no pagination of large group sets.

Dataset.schema is informational only (dataset.rs); rows are schema-on-read. No type validation, no column existence checks against the query, no error on typo'd ORDER BY/GROUP BY columns (silently no-op).

featureGaps.feature: Capability revocation enforcement in the serving agent
featureGaps.notes: serve_cmd (main.rs:338) passes is_revoked as `&|_, _| false`, so on-chain RevokeCapability is never consulted by the map agent — a revoked-but-unexpired token still authorizes queries. Also accepted_roots and self_tags are hardcoded empty.
dataset add fully replaces; no incremental append of shards, no row deletion, no dataset versioning/time-travel (a BigQuery staple).

robustnessIssues.issue: Sequential shard execution under load: engine::run awaits each shard's map serially, so a single slow/timing-out host stalls the whole query for its 30s timeout before the next shard even starts. Combined with no overall query deadline this is a latency/DoS hazard.

No upper bound on dataset size, shard count, result-group cardinality, or per-shard byte size in engine/serve_map. A GROUP BY on a high-cardinality column or a giant shard materializes unbounded memory on both coordinator and serving host (decode_shard returns the whole Vec<Row>). No max-bytes/rows guard.

Error classification by substring-matching error text (contains '404'/'timeout'/'504'). Fragile: SDK wording changes or a body legitimately containing '404' misclassifies MissingBlob vs Timeout vs HostError, changing retry behavior. Should classify on a typed SDK error, not strings.

Map agent reply on encode failure is `serde_json::to_vec(&reply).unwrap_or_default()` — on (extremely unlikely) serialization failure it replies with empty bytes, which the coordinator then tries to deserialize as MapReply and fails as Corrupt; the requester gets a confusing error rather than a clear host error.

Catalog claims 'single-writer' and atomic save, but there is no file lock; two concurrent `ce-query dataset add` invocations race (load-modify-save) and the last writer silently clobbers the other's dataset. No advisory lock or compare-and-swap.

Revocation and roots are stubbed in the serving path: is_revoked is `|_,_| false` and accepted_roots/self_tags are empty, so capability revocation and delegated-root grants are silently non-functional in production despite caps.rs supporting them.

f64 aggregation is order-sensitive at the floating-point level; the monoid 'associativity/commutativity' proptests pass only because test inputs are small integers exactly representable in f64. Real-world SUM/AVG over many fractional values will give run-dependent results, undermining the determinism the design relies on for safe retry/redistribution.

serve loop polls every poll_ms and processes messages sequentially with an awaited get_object+map per request; a slow shard fetch blocks all other inbound map requests on that host (head-of-line blocking). No per-request spawn or concurrency.

No query-level timeout/deadline in engine::run; max_attempts_per_shard bounds host hops but a host that accepts and stalls under the (default 30s) per-request timeout multiplies across attempts and shards with no total cap.

testGaps.area: Concurrent/parallel fan-out behavior and bounded concurrency (once implemented); ordering of out-of-order partial arrival under real async
MeshMapHost end-to-end against a real or in-memory CeClient (request/reply round-trip, timeout, error mapping) — only Local/Mock are exercised; MeshMapHost::map has no test
serve_cmd agent loop: malformed payload, missing reply_token, unauthorized-with-grant, revoked token, multi-request handling — only serve_map's reject path is unit-tested, the loop itself is untested
Catalog concurrent writers (two dataset add racing) and crash-during-rename recovery
f64 aggregation precision/associativity over fractional and large-magnitude values (current proptests only use small integers, hiding the float non-associativity)
CLI end-to-end: dataset add → plan → run round-trip against a node (no e2e/integration test of the binary exists)
Result verification / quorum across redundant hosts (feature absent, so untested) — adversarial host returning a wrong Partial is not detected or tested
join.rs distributed/streaming over the engine (joins are never run through engine::run, so the distributed join path is untested end-to-end)
Large-result / high-cardinality GROUP BY memory bounds and LIMIT-on-huge-group-set behavior

docGaps: No rustdoc doctests beyond the one in lib.rs/README; README claims '1 doctest' but examples in README use `?` in non-async/non-Result context and `users`/`orders` undefined (the join snippet and the sql::parse snippet won't compile as doctests — they are illustrative only).
No CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, or architecture decision record beyond inline module docs (which are, to their credit, excellent).
No examples/ directory and no sample .ndjson dataset, so a new user cannot run the CLI quickstart as written without inventing data.

README overstates maturity vs BigQuery (no mention that projection queries, JOIN-in-SQL, predicate pushdown, cost limits, and quorum verification are absent) — the limitations section only lists subqueries/HAVING.
No documentation of the f64 precision limitation for SUM/AVG, which matters given the project's integer-base-unit money convention.
serve/grant security caveats (revocation hardcoded off, roots empty) are undocumented — an operator would assume --require-grant honors revocation.

ce-mail.name: ce-mail
ce-mail.currentState: ce-mail is a genuinely well-built Rust library (edition 2024) implementing decentralized, identity-native messaging over the CE SDK, with a small CLI. The architecture is clean and layered: crypto.rs (Ed25519->X25519 NaCl-style sealed-box E2E body encryption on curve25519-dalek + ChaCha20-Poly1305), envelope.rs (tiny signed metadata + content-addressed body CID, domain-separated bincode signing), receipt.rs (signed delivery/read receipts), proto.rs (a single serde request/reply enum over ce-rs app-messaging), mailbox.rs (store-and-forward store with idempotent de-dup, bounded retention, and a ce-cap capability gate), service.rs (pure request->store state machine), thread.rs (in_reply_to + subject-normalization threading), client.rs (high-level MailClient with a Transport trait so the full send->store->drain->decrypt flow is unit-testable against an in-memory fake), and main.rs (send/inbox/read/grant-mailbox/serve-mailbox CLI). What genuinely works end-to-end: signed envelopes, E2E sealed bodies, store-and-forward with cursor drain + pagination + ack, idempotent delivery, capability-gated open-relay protection, signed receipts, and threading. What is documented-but-NOT-implemented: postage/anti-spam enforcement (the envelope field and threat-model exist but no minimum-postage check, no payment-channel wiring, no refund flow), screening/allowlists/contacts, the SMTP gateway (docs/smtp-gateway.md only), attachments (attachment_cids exists on the wire but SendOptions cannot set them and open_body never fetches them), and subject encryption. No unwrap()/expect()/panic!/todo!/unimplemented! exist in any production path — all are confined to #[cfg(test)] modules. Logging in the long-running mailbox loop uses eprintln! rather than tracing, violating project standards.
plan: Make mailbox persistence crash-safe and efficient: replace the per-request full-store std::fs::write with an atomic temp-file + rename, batch/throttle writes (or append a small WAL), and fsync. This is the highest-severity production risk (data loss for all recipients).

Implement attachments end-to-end: add attachments: Vec<Vec<u8>> (or Vec<(name, bytes)>) to SendOptions, seal+store each as a blob, populate attachment_cids, and add MailClient::open_attachment(envelope, index) that lazily fetches+decrypts. Add e2e + property tests. This closes the biggest gap vs. the claimed feature set.

Add protocol-level resource bounds: enforce a max envelope/subject/body-CID-count and a server-side max DrainPage limit; reject oversized Deliver payloads in MailService::handle before storing. Add property tests for the bounds. Closes the DoS/memory-amplification holes.

Implement postage anti-spam enforcement: define a recipient ScreeningPolicy (allowlist/contacts + require-postage-from-strangers), verify the postage_receipt as a real payment-channel receipt via ce-rs, and gate delivery/surfacing on it. Wire the refund-on-not-spam path. This realizes the threat-model's central promise.

Add screening/allowlists/contacts and an is_newcomer()/history-based prioritization using GET /history/:node_id, plus a spam vs inbox separation in the client. Test allowlist-waives-postage and stranger-requires-postage.

Refresh revocation periodically in serve-mailbox (re-fetch ce.revoked() on an interval or per-N requests) so on-chain revocation actually takes effect on long-running mailboxes; add an integration test for revoke-after-start.

Replace eprintln!/println! in serve_mailbox and library-adjacent paths with tracing per project standards; switch the mailbox loop from a 500ms poll to a streaming subscription if ce-rs exposes one, with bounded backoff on errors.

Add concurrency hardening + tests: wrap MailboxStore for concurrent access (the node serves requests concurrently) and add a multi-threaded test exercising interleaved deliver/drain/ack to prove no lost-update or double-evict.

Surface encode/serialize failures instead of unwrap_or_default(): make encode()/to_bytes() return Result (or at minimum log + propagate) so a serialization failure never silently produces an empty message/store.

Build the SMTP gateway MVP (inbound relay -> envelope, outbound CE->SMTP with address mapping) per docs/smtp-gateway.md, clearly scoped as non-E2E for legacy interop, with integration tests against a local SMTP sink.

Add CLI + CeTransport integration tests (currently only the in-memory fake is covered) and add multi-recipient/CC modeling. Update README/rustdoc to mark unimplemented features explicitly and add a runnable doctest.

featureGaps.feature: Attachments (functional, not just a wire field)
featureGaps.notes: EnvelopeBody.attachment_cids exists and is signed, but SendOptions has no attachments field, send() always sets attachment_cids: vec![], and Message/open_body never seals, stores, or fetches attachment blobs. Gmail's core attach/download is entirely unimplemented despite docs claiming lazy 40MB fetch.

postage_receipt is a pass-through opaque string; nothing validates it, requires it, locks credits, or refunds. The threat-model explicitly marks this as 'next milestone'. Without it the spam story is aspirational. Need a recipient screening policy that requires postage from strangers and a payment-channel receipt verifier.
No address book, no allow-cap, no is_newcomer()/history-based prioritization, no spam vs inbox separation, no block/mute. Gmail's filtering and categorization are absent entirely.
docs/smtp-gateway.md only; zero code. No inbound/outbound bridge, no address mapping, no SPF/DKIM handling. Gmail is fundamentally an SMTP endpoint.
Subject is cleartext metadata. No option to seal subjects for known contacts. Gmail TLS-protects everything in transit.

featureGaps.feature: Search / labels / folders / read-unread state / star/archive
featureGaps.notes: Inbox is a flat drained list saved to inbox.json; no full-text search, no labels, no folders, no per-message flags beyond cryptographic receipts. The 'read' command does prefix lookup over the last snapshot only.
EnvelopeBody.to is a single NodeId. No CC/BCC, no group/list addressing, no fan-out. Each recipient needs a separately sealed body (per-recipient ECDH) which is not modeled.

featureGaps.feature: Drafts, outbox, send-retry, scheduled send, undo send
featureGaps.notes: send() is fire-and-forget; no draft persistence, no outbox queue, no retry/backoff on transient mesh failures, no scheduled or delayed send.

Mailbox store is in-memory HashMap, persisted only by rewriting the whole file on every request in serve-mailbox. No replication, no fsync/atomic-write, no WAL; a crash mid-write can corrupt the store. Gmail is highly available and durable.

featureGaps.feature: Rich content (HTML bodies, inline images, MIME)
featureGaps.notes: Bodies are opaque bytes rendered as UTF-8 lossy text; no MIME, content-type, or HTML rendering model.

robustnessIssues.issue: Whole-store rewrite on every request with no atomic write or fsync: serve_mailbox does std::fs::write(p, svc.store().to_bytes()) after each handled message. A crash or disk-full mid-write truncates/corrupts the entire mailbox (all recipients' mail). Should write to a temp file + rename, and avoid O(n) full-serialization per message.

No byte-size bounds on envelopes, subjects, or the Deliver payload. capacity_per_recipient bounds message COUNT, not bytes. A single envelope can carry an arbitrarily large subject string (it is cleartext and signed but unbounded), and DrainPage limit is usize with no server-side max, so a malicious or buggy client/peer can request a huge page. DoS / memory-amplification vector.

Revocation set is fetched once at mailbox startup (ce.revoked()) and captured in a move closure; it never refreshes. A capability revoked after the mailbox starts is still honored until restart, defeating on-chain revocation for long-running mailboxes.

serve-mailbox uses a 500ms poll loop over ce.messages() instead of an event/stream subscription; under load this both adds latency and can drop/lag. Also messages() failure only sleeps 2s and retries with no backoff cap or circuit breaking.

Deliver does not bind the authenticated requester to envelope.body.from. This is defensible (envelopes are self-authenticating, so relays may forward), but it means there is no per-sender rate limiting or accountability at the mailbox — combined with no postage enforcement, a holder of a valid accept-grant (or the recipient's own delegate) can flood the recipient's bounded queue, silently evicting legitimate mail via capacity eviction.

encode()/to_bytes()/encode_sealed() swallow bincode errors with unwrap_or_default(), producing empty byte vectors on failure. An empty encode silently corrupts a message/store rather than surfacing an error; decode then fails downstream far from the cause.

x25519_public_from_node_id accepts any 32-byte point that decompresses; no small-subgroup / low-order point check before ECDH. With ephemeral-static sealing this is low-risk, but a hardened impl should reject low-order recipient points to avoid invalid-curve edge cases.
Inbox snapshot (inbox.json) and cursor are written non-atomically and the cursor advances even if save_snapshot partially fails; a crash can desync the saved cursor from what 'read' can see.

testGaps.area: Mailbox store-corruption / partial-write recovery in serve-mailbox
Revocation refresh while a mailbox is running (revoked-after-start grant must be rejected)
Large-payload / oversized-subject / huge DrainPage limit DoS bounds
Concurrent delivery + drain + ack against one MailboxStore (the real node will have concurrent requests; store is sync and tests are single-threaded)
Attachments end-to-end (none exist because the feature is unimplemented)
Postage enforcement / refund flow (unimplemented, untested)
CLI commands (send/inbox/read/grant-mailbox/serve-mailbox) and snapshot/cursor persistence
CeTransport real-node path (only the in-memory FakeNet/Handle is exercised; ce-rs CeClient calls are untested)
Cross-version persistence beyond the v1->v2 legacy case (forward-compat / corrupted-magic handling)
Low-order / invalid recipient point handling in seal()

docGaps: README and module docs describe attachments, postage enforcement, and the SMTP gateway as if they work; they are unimplemented. Docs should clearly mark these as not-yet-implemented to avoid overclaiming (the threat-model is honest about postage, but lib.rs/envelope.rs read as if attachments are functional).
No rustdoc doctests are runnable: the lib.rs example is #[no_run] and uses a placeholder recipient id; there are no compile-tested examples of the receipt or threading APIs.

No architecture/sequence doc for the mailbox request loop, persistence model, or the capability-gate authorization derivation (the mailbox.rs module comment is good but there is no top-level ARCHITECTURE.md or diagram).
No documentation of operational limits (max body/subject size, capacity semantics = count not bytes, poll interval, persistence durability guarantees).
CLI help is present but there is no usage walkthrough in README for the full offline flow (grant-mailbox -> serve-mailbox -> send --mailbox -> inbox --ack).

ce-meet.name: ce-meet
ce-meet.currentState: ce-meet is a Rust library plus thin CLI implementing only the SIGNALING plane of a Meet-like app on the CE mesh; the media plane (audio/video) is explicitly out of scope (browser WebRTC). The signaling logic is genuinely well-built and pure. proto.rs defines a sequence-numbered SignalEnvelope wrapping a Signal enum (Join/Leave/Keepalive/Offer/Answer/IceCandidate/IceEnd). room.rs implements a clean LWW-per-member CRDT roster with convergence, snapshot/restore, merge, and liveness pruning. order.rs is a per-peer reorder buffer (OrderedInbox/SignalRouter) giving in-order de-duplicated SDP/ICE delivery with a bounded window. caps.rs plus admit.rs implement capability-gated admission over ce-cap with a MAC'd, identity-bound ResumeToken and constant-time comparison. turn.rs defines IceServer/TurnCredential config types with channel-bound ephemeral TURN-REST credential derivation. client.rs (MeetClient) and main.rs (CLI: create-room/join/signal/host) wire it to the ce-rs SDK; all SDK methods used were verified to exist. What works: the entire signaling/roster/admission/ordering state machine, exercised by ~70 unit/integration/property tests. What is documentation-only/stubbed: the TURN coturn sidecar, the credential-issuance endpoint, the SFU cell, relay discovery wiring (find_service referenced only in docs), and ALL media-control signaling (mute, screen-share, recording, chat, reactions, raise-hand). No unwrap/expect/panic/todo in any non-test path. The CLI is a 1s poll loop, not SSE-driven real-time.
plan: Add in-call media-control signaling: extend Signal with Mute{audio,video}, ScreenShare{active}, and optionally RaiseHand/Reaction/Chat broadcast variants; thread mute/camera/sharing state into Member and surface as new Effect variants. Highest-leverage gap and fits the existing envelope/CRDT model directly.

Add bounds/DoS guards: configurable max-members cap in Room, max-peers cap on SignalRouter.inboxes, and max-payload plus max-SDP/candidate-length checks in from_bytes/Signal validation. Add failure-path tests for each.
Switch the CLI and provide a library helper to drive MeetClient::ingest_ordered from the ce-rs SSE message stream in real time instead of the 1s/500ms poll loop; keep poll as fallback.

Implement host moderation: Kick/RemoveParticipant and EndRoom signals gated by ABILITY_MODERATE/ABILITY_HOST through the Gate, a lobby/waiting-room admission mode, and mute-others. Wire the currently-unused ability constants into enforcement.

Build the first real media tier: wire relay discovery (find_service(SERVICE_TURN), rank by atlas/RTT) into the Admitter so AdmitResp returns live TURN IceServers, and implement the channel-bound TURN credential issuance path (coturn sidecar + REST endpoint). Treat SFU as a later milestone.
Replace both MAC constructions (admit.rs resume_mac, turn.rs derive) with HMAC-SHA256 or keyed blake3, and make turn.rs verify use constant-time comparison like admit.rs ct_eq.

Enforce or remove the sent_at freshness claim: implement a configurable freshness window that discards stale directed signals (and document it), or strip the misleading guarantee from proto.rs/room.rs docs.

Add integration tests with a mock CeClient covering MeetClient.poll/subscribe/request_admission and the host admit-reply loop, plus a CLI e2e test (create-room -> host -> join -> signal). Add admit-loop rate limiting and a flood-resilience test.
Add an examples/ directory (two-party signaling walkthrough) and an architecture/sequence doc; add an explicit Implemented-vs-Planned section to README and turn.rs.
Add property tests for OrderedInbox under extreme/wraparound seqs and for the new bounds (members/peers/payload); add RecordingStarted/Stopped consent signaling if recording is in scope.

featureGaps.feature: Actual media plane (TURN relay server + SFU cell)
featureGaps.notes: Only config TYPES exist (IceServer, TurnCredential). The coturn sidecar, the channel-bound TURN credential issuance endpoint, relay discovery wiring (find_service(meet:turn) is documented in turn.rs but never called), and the SFU cell image are all absent. Symmetric-NAT peers and large rooms cannot connect. Google Meet is fundamentally an SFU.

featureGaps.feature: In-call media-control signaling: mute/unmute, camera on/off, screen-share start/stop
featureGaps.notes: The Signal enum has no Mute, VideoState, or ScreenShare variants. Meet shows live mute/camera/sharing state per participant; ce-meet roster has no such concept.
No recording primitive at all. Even signaling RecordingStarted/Stopped to the roster is missing.
No Chat/Reaction/RaiseHand signal. These fit the existing broadcast envelope model trivially but are unmodeled.
main.rs join/host loops poll ce.messages() on a fixed timer (1000ms/500ms). WebRTC needs sub-second signaling; docs reference SSE and ingest_ordered but the CLI never uses them.

featureGaps.feature: Moderation: kick/remove, end-for-all, lobby/waiting room, mute-others
featureGaps.notes: ABILITY_MODERATE and ABILITY_HOST constants exist but no Kick/EndRoom/MuteParticipant signal or enforcement path uses them. The Admitter only does the join-gate, no eject.

featureGaps.feature: Participant metadata beyond name (role, connection quality, speaking)
featureGaps.notes: Member carries only display_name + presence; no role (host/cohost), connection-quality, or speaking indicator.
Rooms are ephemeral random ids; no calendar integration, human-readable names, or PSTN dial-in.
Identity is authenticated at signaling but DTLS-SRTP fingerprints carried in SDP are not independently verified or surfaced by the app.

robustnessIssues.issue: No upper bound on roster size. Room.members (HashMap) grows unbounded; a peer can publish Join envelopes with many distinct from-NodeIds with no cap, enabling memory-exhaustion DoS. Add a max-members limit.

No upper bound on SignalRouter.inboxes (one OrderedInbox per distinct from). A flood of directed signals from many senders allocates unbounded per-peer buffers; each buffer is window-bounded but the count is not.

No size/sanity validation on SDP or ICE-candidate bodies. Offer.sdp/Answer.sdp/IceCandidate.candidate are arbitrary-length Strings forwarded verbatim; from_bytes has no max-payload guard. A peer can publish multi-megabyte blobs.

sent_at freshness is documented but never enforced. Room::apply and OrderedInbox ignore sent_at entirely (only seq is used), so stale-but-higher-seq directed signals are delivered. Either implement a freshness window or stop documenting the guarantee.

Resume-token and TURN MAC use raw SHA256(secret||fields), not HMAC. NUL-separated and domain-prefixed, mitigating trivial length-extension, but a true keyed MAC (HMAC-SHA256/keyed blake3) is the correct primitive.
turn.rs TurnCredential::verify compares password with a plain == on hex strings, unlike admit.rs which uses constant-time ct_eq; inconsistent and a minor timing side-channel.
to_bytes() swallows serialization errors and silently emits an empty-object body on failure; for these plain types it is infallible, but the fallback masks a bug rather than surfacing it.
No rate limiting on admission requests. The host runs full ce-cap signature verification for every meet:admit message each poll; an attacker can flood admit requests to burn host CPU.

Host reply uses serde_json::to_vec(&resp).unwrap_or_default(); on the impossible error it sends an empty body the joiner cannot parse, surfacing as a generic transport error instead of an explicit denial.

testGaps.area: MeetClient async I/O paths (poll, subscribe, request_admission, send_admit) against a mock CeClient; only pure apply/ingest are unit-tested
CLI commands (create-room, join, signal, host) end-to-end including the host admit/reply round trip
Roster/router memory bounds under a flood of distinct senders (no cap exists, so no test asserts one)
Concurrent host plus multiple joiners admission race / resume-token reuse across overlapping reconnects
Oversized/adversarial SDP and candidate payloads (size, control chars, non-SDP garbage) reaching apply
sent_at freshness behavior (unused field; a property test would expose the doc/behavior mismatch)
OrderedInbox window force-advance under adversarial seq patterns near u64::MAX wraparound
TurnCredential::verify timing side-channel and the plain-equality password compare

docGaps: README/rustdoc are thorough but oversell: turn.rs, the README media-tier table, and lib.rs describe TURN-via-relay, SFU cells, and relay discovery as if integrated when they are config-types-only with no wiring. Add an explicit Implemented-vs-Planned boundary.
No examples/ directory despite CLI categories; the only runnable example is the lib.rs no_run doctest. Add a two-party signaling walkthrough.
No architecture/sequence doc for the join handshake (request_admission -> Admitter -> AdmitResp -> subscribe -> announce_join -> SDP/ICE) which spans 4 files.
No documentation of the security rationale for the non-HMAC SHA256(secret||...) MAC construction.
sent_at is documented as a freshness guarantee in proto.rs and room.rs but is never enforced (actively misleading).
No CHANGELOG and no note that the CLI poll loop is unsuitable for real WebRTC timing.

ce-notes.name: ce-notes
ce-notes.currentState: ce-notes is a single Rust crate (edition 2024) implementing local-first, E2E-encrypted CRDT notes as a CE app over the ce-rs/ce-coord SDK, plus a CLI binary. Architecture is clean and genuinely layered: crypto.rs (XChaCha20-Poly1305 space key + X25519 sealed-box key wrapping, Ed25519->X25519 derivation), notedoc.rs (yrs/Yjs text CRDT behind a NoteDoc trait), mergelog.rs (one writer-log + N reader-logs per space, set-union of commutative ops over ce-coord), store.rs (at-rest sealed persistence), model.rs (SpaceMeta/MemberEntry/Role/NoteHeader/Folder/AttachmentRef/NoteOp/Invite), and notes.rs (the Notes/Space API). What genuinely works and is well-tested: the crypto envelope, key wrap/unwrap, deterministic X25519-from-NodeId derivation, CRDT convergence/idempotence, sealed at-rest storage, and the invite/import (ce-cap grant + wrapped-key handoff) path. There are NO todo!/unimplemented!/panic! and no unwrap/expect in production paths (all unwraps are in tests). Relative to Google Keep, however, the actual note model is just title + markdown body + folders + attachments: there is no search, no labels/tags, no pin/archive, no color, no checklists, and no reminders. Key rotation/revoke is documented-but-unimplemented, reader-role write-protection is not enforced app-side, the index is an unbounded append-only JSON-in-Y.Text log, and cross-device realtime sync relies on a 250ms poll with the applied/drained high-water marks never persisted.
plan: Fix the text-CRDT indexing bug: make notedoc::diff/set_text operate in UTF-16 code units (yrs's index space) instead of char scalars, and add a property test with emoji/non-BMP strings asserting convergence and exact round-trip. This is a silent data-corruption bug and the highest-leverage correctness fix.

Add the Keep-defining note attributes to NoteHeader and the model: pinned, archived, color, and a labels Vec<LabelId> plus a Label type; update notes() to pin-sort, hide archived, and add list-by-label/list-archived/list-trash + restore. Expose CLI subcommands (pin/unpin, archive/unarchive, color, label add/rm, trash ls/restore).

Implement structured checklists: a checklist note variant backed by a Y.Array of {id, text, checked, order} items with CRDT-safe add/check/reorder, distinct from the freeform markdown body, plus CLI commands (check/uncheck/add-item).

Add local full-text search: maintain an in-memory (and optionally on-disk sealed) inverted index over decrypted titles+bodies+labels, with a `ce-notes search <space> <query>` command; rebuild it in rebuild_index_state and update on set_note_text.
Enforce roles app-side: gate create_note/set_note_text/delete_note/create_folder behind meta.can_write(self.node_id) and return a typed error for readers; add failure-path tests.

Implement key rotation / revoke: Space::revoke(member) -> bump key_epoch, generate a new space key, re-wrap to every remaining active member, persist, and seal new ops under the new epoch; add an integration test that a revoked member cannot decrypt post-rotation ops while remaining members can.

Compact the index: replace the append-only JSON-in-Y.Text log with a structured CRDT (Y.Map per id, or periodic snapshot+truncate) so per-edit cost is O(1) and storage is bounded; add a growth/compaction test.
Persist and use the applied/drained high-water marks (wire Store::save_applied/load_applied into MergeSet open/pump) so reopen does not re-drain the whole history; add a resume-after-restart test.

Add bounds/limits and DoS guards: max body size, max attachment size with streaming/chunked encryption instead of whole-file read-into-RAM, max members, and a max decoded size for Invite/op deserialization; tests for over-limit rejection.
Replace the silent decrypt-failure swallow in apply_incoming with structured logging/metrics (tracing::warn with op.doc_id/epoch) distinguishing expected cross-epoch skips from unexpected failures.

Add reminders: a reminder field on NoteHeader (time-based first), persisted in the index CRDT, plus a `ce-notes reminders` listing of due/upcoming notes (notification delivery can be a later integration).

Add a real two-node e2e test (spawn two ce nodes, A and B, share via invite, assert cross-device convergence and offline-merge) wired into CI behind the existing CE_BIN gate, since live_node.rs currently cannot assert convergence.

Make the lib.rs doctest executable (drop no_run where feasible or add a compile-checked example under examples/) and add a CONTRIBUTING/threat-model doc; correct store.rs docs to match actual on-disk files.

featureGaps.notes: Google Keep's core UX is instant search over note text, labels, and types. There is no search command or Space method at all; notes() only lists headers sorted by updated_at. Need an index over decrypted body text (local-only, since mesh is ciphertext).

Keep is organized by colored labels, not just folders. model.rs has only Folder (single-parent tree) and NoteHeader has folder_id but no labels. Add a labels Vec<LabelId> to NoteHeader + a Label type + list-by-label filtering.
Keep pins notes to the top and archives them out of the main list. NoteHeader has no pinned/archived flags; notes() cannot pin-sort or hide archived. Add bool fields + sort/filter.

featureGaps.feature: Checklists (structured list items with checked state)
featureGaps.notes: A defining Keep feature. Body is a single freeform markdown Y.Text; there is no structured checklist model, no per-item checkbox state, no reordering. Needs a list-item CRDT (Y.Array of items) or a structured body type.
Keep notifies you at a time/place. No reminder field, no scheduling, no notification path anywhere in the model or CLI.
Keep color-codes notes. NoteHeader has no color field; trivially missing.

Documented as unimplemented (Space::revoke does not exist). MemberEntry has a revoked tombstone and SpaceMeta.key_epoch exists, but nothing rotates the space key or re-wraps to remaining members, so revoking a member does not actually cut off their access to future content.

Role::Reader exists and abilities() attenuates, but Space::set_note_text/delete_note/create_note never check meta.can_write(self). A local Reader can still publish write-ops to its writer-log; only a remote host enforcing the cap would reject them, and the app does not gate locally.

Keep supports inline images and basic formatting. Bodies are plain markdown text; attachments are not linked into the body structurally (linkage is a freeform marker line, per the code comment in attach()).
delete_note is a tombstone (good, recoverable in principle) but there is no command to list trashed notes, restore, or empty trash; deleted notes simply vanish from notes().
Keep shows collaborators; CRDT substrate could support presence but none is implemented.
Only a CLI exists; README notes the TUI and web client are unbuilt. Keep is primarily GUI/mobile.

robustnessIssues.issue: set_text diff computes prefix/suffix/removed lengths in Unicode char (scalar) units, but yrs Text indexes by UTF-16 code units. For any note containing non-BMP characters (emoji, many CJK supplementary, etc.) the remove_range/insert offsets are wrong, corrupting the CRDT delta and the converged text. The code comment acknowledges this but ships it.

The per-space index is an append-only newline-delimited JSON log stored inside a single Y.Text; every header upsert (every title edit, delete, folder change) appends a full JSON line and rebuild_index_state re-parses the entire text on every change. This grows unbounded and is O(n) per edit and O(n) memory — a DoS/bloat vector for large or long-lived notebooks. No compaction.

applied/drained high-water marks are never persisted. Store::save_applied/load_applied exist but notes.rs never calls them; MergeSet.drained always starts at 0 on open. On every reopen the app re-drains and re-applies the entire op history (decrypt + CRDT apply for every op ever published). Correct for LWW index but wastefully O(history) and silently divergent from the documented store layout.

No size/length limits anywhere: note bodies, attachment file size (std::fs::read of an arbitrary path into memory), index log, and number of members are all unbounded. A large attachment is read fully into RAM and sealed in one buffer; a malicious/huge invite or op is decoded without limits.
robustnessIssues.location: src/core/notes.rs:391-413 (attach reads whole file), 549-557 (apply_index_update), model.rs:186-194 (Invite::decode serde_json unbounded)

robustnessIssues.issue: Reader role is not enforced on the write path: set_note_text/create_note/delete_note do not consult meta.can_write(self.node_id). A member holding only Role::Reader can still author and publish write-ops locally.
robustnessIssues.location: src/core/notes.rs:306-365 (create/set/delete), model.rs:98-100 (can_write defined but unused on writes)

robustnessIssues.issue: apply_incoming silently swallows any op whose decryption fails (returns Ok(())). This is intended for cross-epoch ops, but it also masks genuinely corrupt/forged ops and key-rotation bugs with no logging/metric, making divergence undiagnosable.

store_clone() uses self.store.root().parent().unwrap_or(root) to recover the data_dir; if root has no parent (root path edge case) it silently re-opens the store at the wrong directory rather than erroring.

x25519_public_from_node_id can fail for NodeIds that are not valid Ed25519 points; invite() will then error, but there is no validation that the target is a real, point-on-curve NodeId before mutating membership/persisting — partial state could be written if a later step fails (no transactionality across save_meta + add_peer).
robustnessIssues.location: src/core/notes.rs:436-496 (invite mutates meta/persists then adds peer)

robustnessIssues.issue: now_secs() and note id construction format!("{:016x}{}", now_secs(), random_hex(8)) rely on wall-clock for ordering; clock skew/backwards time produces mis-ordered ids, and two notes created in the same second differ only by 8 random bytes (fine) but updated_at ties are broken only by note_id string compare.

testGaps.area: Multi-device convergence over a real mesh (two nodes, A writes / B reads / both edit offline then merge)
live_node.rs explicitly does NOT assert cross-device convergence (single node, two coord handles share one inbox ring); the actual peer-sync path is untested in CI
Non-BMP / emoji / multi-byte text through set_text -> CRDT delta -> converge (would catch the UTF-16 vs char indexing bug)
Index growth / compaction behavior and rebuild correctness after many upserts and deletes of the same note
Reader-role write rejection (currently no enforcement, so no test could pass) and Owner-only invite/revoke authorization
Key rotation / revoke round-trip: after rotation, revoked member cannot decrypt new ops, remaining members can
Concurrent local writers: many tokio tasks calling set_note_text/pump_once on one Space simultaneously (mutex ordering, no deadlock, no lost ops)
Attachment fetch when the cached plaintext is corrupt or size-mismatched, and when get_object returns tampered ciphertext
applied/drained persistence and resume-after-restart (currently the code path is dead, so untestable as written)
Invite tamper: a grant_token or wrapped_key altered in transit must be rejected on import

docGaps: README 'Status' honestly lists stubs (revoke, checkpointing, TUI, SSE, attachment linkage) but does NOT disclose the UTF-16/char indexing correctness risk or that reader-role is unenforced app-side.
No architecture/design doc in-repo (README references 'design doc §5-6' and milestones M1-M5 that are not present in the crate); the referenced spec is external/missing.
No rustdoc doctests are executable: the lib.rs example is no_run, so the public API surface is never compile-checked against doc drift.
No CONTRIBUTING or threat-model doc spelling out the confidentiality boundary, what the mesh sees, and the documented revocation/forward-secrecy tradeoff beyond a single README paragraph.
Store layout doc in store.rs mentions applied.json as part of the format, but the code never writes it — docs describe an unimplemented behavior.
No examples/ directory; the only runnable demos are the two-device shell/PowerShell scripts, which require external running nodes and are not covered by CI.

ce-chat.name: ce-chat
ce-chat.currentState: ce-chat is a Vite + TypeScript browser SPA (no framework, hand-rolled DOM helpers) that maps each chat channel/DM to a CE mesh pubsub topic and rides the @ce-net/sdk `CeClient.mesh` (subscribe/publish + one SSE `streamMessages` stream). The architecture is clean and genuinely decoupled: a narrow `MeshLike` port lets all core logic (protocol envelopes, topic mapping, dedupe store, presence TTL, error mapping, ChatService data path) be unit-tested against an in-memory fake mesh with no node. What truly works: public channels, capability-gated private channels (subscribe 403 -> friendly grant prompt), order-independent DMs derived from sorted node ids, optimistic send with echo-confirm/dedupe, heartbeat-driven presence with TTL aging, a 500-message ring cap per channel, and a polished UI (sidebar, roster, composer, modals, skeleton/offline states, linkify with no innerHTML injection). There are no `todo!()`/`unimplemented!()` and no mock data in the data path — only the test fake. Real gaps: presence only heartbeats into the *active* channel (peers in background channels never see you), no message persistence/history backfill (a fresh join shows an empty channel), and the entire feature set is "send/receive plain text + presence" — no threads, reactions, mentions, typing indicators, read receipts, or editing/deletion.
plan: Fix the dedupe-spoofing hazard: key the store and all dedupe on the tuple (from, id) instead of bare id, and on receive namespace the stored key by frame.from so a hostile/duplicate id from one peer cannot suppress another author's message or a victim's own send. Add a regression test.

Add message history backfill: define a `history-request`/`history-response` envelope (or use node durable storage if the SDK exposes it), so a node joining a channel asks peers for the last N messages and renders scrollback instead of an empty pane. This is the single biggest gap vs Slack/Chat.

Fan presence heartbeats out to ALL joined channels (not just app.active) on the heartbeat timer, so members stay online across background channels; add a test that a peer subscribed to two channels stays online in both.

Add automatic SSE stream reconnect with exponential backoff + jitter inside ChatService (cap, abortable), surfacing a transient 'reconnecting' state and auto-recovering live updates without a full boot()/reload; test recovery after an injected drop.
Persist the joined-channel list and DMs to localStorage and rehydrate on boot (instead of only the 3 hardcoded DEFAULT_CHANNELS), and persist per-channel composer drafts; test rehydration.

Implement reactions: add a `react` envelope ({targetId, emoji, op}), aggregate reaction counts in the store keyed by target message, and render a reaction bar + picker. Cover add/remove/dedupe in tests.
Implement threads: add an optional `replyTo` field to ChatMsg, group replies, and add a thread side-pane; keep root-channel rendering unchanged when replyTo is absent.

Implement @mentions + unread/notification model: parse @name/@nodeId tokens in linkify, highlight mentions of self, and track per-channel unread counts + a last-read marker rendered as badges in the sidebar.
Add typing indicators via a throttled `typing` envelope (rate-limited, TTL'd) and a transient roster/composer overlay; ensure it never persists to the message log.

Harden input validation and DoS bounds: length-cap the envelope `name` field in parseEnvelope, add per-sender inbound rate limiting, debounce/batch renderStream on bursts (rAF coalescing), and cap deeply nested/huge JSON before parse.

Replace the UI-only `failed` Set with a first-class message state in StoredMessage (status: pending|sent|failed) owned by the store/service, with a retry action that re-publishes; test the publish-failure path end to end.
Remove the non-null `app!`/DOM `!` assertions in main.ts by introducing guarded accessors that no-op when app is null or a node is missing, eliminating the race-throw on reconnect/reload.
Make the node URL configurable (localStorage/env override of DEFAULT_NODE_URL) so the app can target a remote or alternate-port node without editing source.

Add tests for the currently-untested layers: dom.ts (linkify boundaries + injection safety), sdk-adapter.ts (mesh frame normalization, safeUtf8 on invalid bytes), and a real two-node e2e harness behind an opt-in flag; add property tests for store ordering/eviction invariants.
Write ARCHITECTURE.md (MeshLike port, reducer/dedupe model, protocol versioning/compat policy) and a README Limitations section enumerating unsupported features and surprising behaviors.

featureGaps.notes: A newly-joined member sees an empty channel — pubsub only delivers live frames. There is no archive, no `since` query, no peer-served history. Real Slack/Chat always shows scrollback. Needs a history-request envelope answered by peers, or node-side durable storage.
ChatMsg has no parentId/threadId. No threaded view. Slack/Chat threads are core. Add a `replyTo` field to the envelope and a thread pane.
No reaction envelope type, no per-message reaction aggregation in the store. Add a `react` envelope ({targetId, emoji, op:add/remove}) and reducer state.
No mention parsing, no highlight, no unread/notification badge. linkify only handles URLs. Add @-token parsing keyed on node id/name and an unread-count model.
Listed in the reference set but absent. Needs a throttled `typing` heartbeat envelope and a transient roster overlay.
No per-channel unread tracking, no last-read marker, no receipt envelope. Sidebar shows online counts but never unread badges.
Envelopes are immutable; no edit/tombstone type. Real products allow edit/delete with an edited marker.
main.ts heartbeats only into app.active, so members of non-active channels age out and appear offline to peers even though the node is subscribed. Heartbeat should fan out to all joined channels.
Joined channels beyond the 3 hardcoded DEFAULT_CHANNELS are lost on reload — no localStorage of the channel list. Custom channels/DMs vanish on refresh.
Text-only. No CE blob integration despite the SDK likely exposing blobs. Slack/Chat support uploads + previews (formatBytes exists but is unused, hinting this was scoped out).
Only bare-URL linkify. No bold/italic/code-fence rendering, no emoji shortcodes.
No message search across channels.
service.leave() keeps the node subscription (SDK has no unsubscribe); a node accumulates topic subscriptions unboundedly over a long session.

robustnessIssues.issue: Many non-null assertions on `app!` and DOM queries in render/handlers (`const a = app!`, `service.joined().find(...)!`, `service.state(a.active)!`). If a render races a teardown/reconnect (boot() reassigns app, location.reload), these throw uncaught. App-layer convention bans unwrap in prod paths; these are the TS equivalent.

No rate limiting / flood control on inbound frames or the store: a malicious or buggy peer can publish unlimited messages; each unique id is stored (ring-capped at 500 per channel, fine) but onMessages fires a full renderStream() per accepted frame — a burst causes unbounded synchronous re-renders (UI DoS). No per-sender throttle.

Message id is fully attacker-controlled (env.id from the wire).
