A hostile peer can pre-publish a message with the SAME id a victim will optimistically generate, or spoof another author's id, causing the victim's real send to be silently deduped as 'duplicate' and dropped. ids should be namespaced by `from` on receive, or dedupe keyed on (from,id).

Sender identity is fully trusted from frame.from but the display `name` is attacker-supplied and unsanitized/unbounded — a peer can impersonate any display name and ship a 4000-char name (only text is length-capped, name is not). No length cap on name in parseEnvelope.

Stream error handling has no reconnect/backoff: onStreamError surfaces a banner and the consume loop sets streaming=false and exits. The user must manually click Reconnect (which calls boot() and rebuilds everything). A transient SSE drop permanently kills live updates until manual action.

newMessageId uses Math.random salt + a module-global nonceCounter shared across all ChatService instances; collision-resistant in practice but not cryptographically unique and the global counter is a shared-mutable-state hazard if two services coexist (e.g. tests, or future multi-identity).

send() adds the optimistic message and fires onMessages BEFORE awaiting publish; if publish throws, the pending message stays in the store and is only marked failed via a *separate* Set in main.ts (a.failed), which the store/service know nothing about — failed state is UI-only and lost on any rebuild/reload.
location.reload() is used as the mechanism to apply a display-name change (rebuildServiceWithName), discarding all in-memory history and presence. Heavy-handed and data-losing for a routine action.
composerValue is a module-global string shared across channels; selectChannel clears it but a re-render mid-typing or two composers could desync. No per-channel draft preservation.
DEFAULT_NODE_URL falls back to http://127.0.0.1:8844 with no configurability (no env/localStorage override) — can't point at a remote node or alternate port without editing source.

testGaps.area: main.ts (entire UI/render/boot/modal/timer layer, ~780 lines) has zero tests
sdk-adapter.ts (meshAdapter, fetchIdentity, safeUtf8) is untested — the one module touching the real SDK
dom.ts el()/clear()/linkify() untested; linkify URL-boundary and XSS-safety (no innerHTML) deserve explicit assertions
Stream reconnect/backoff behavior — there is no reconnect logic and no test asserting recovery after a transient drop
Duplicate/spoofed message id from a hostile peer (dedupe-griefing) — no test that two different senders using the same id are kept distinct
Concurrency: interleaved frames from many peers, optimistic send racing its own echo under load, ordering stability under burst
Property tests for store ordering invariants (sort stability, eviction never drops a kept message out of order) and normalizeChannelName idempotence
Two+ node end-to-end against a real ce node (only the in-memory fake is exercised; no e2e harness)
Oversize/abusive name field, deeply nested JSON, and very large payloads through parseEnvelope (DoS bounds)

docGaps: No architecture/design doc beyond README; the MeshLike port boundary and the reducer/dedupe model deserve a short ARCHITECTURE.md.
No JSDoc on the public ChatService methods describing failure modes (e.g. that send() leaves a pending message on publish failure, that join() throws on 403).
No documented wire-protocol spec/versioning policy for the envelope (PROTOCOL_VERSION exists but there's no doc on how v2 migration/compat is handled).

README claims a feature set close to Slack ('a tiny Slack') but does not enumerate what is intentionally NOT supported (threads/reactions/history) — a Limitations section is needed to set expectations.
No examples/doctests; no CONTRIBUTING; CHANGELOG absent (single 'Initial commit').
No note that custom channels/DMs are not persisted across reload, nor that presence only covers the active channel — both are surprising behaviors users will hit.

ce-storage.name: ce-storage
ce-storage.currentState: ce-storage is a real, working S3/GCS-subset object store implemented as a CE SDK app over ce-rs blobs. Architecture: src/index.rs holds a BTreeMap-backed bucket index (bucket -> {key -> ObjectMeta}) persisted as pretty JSON via atomic temp-file rename; src/store.rs maps S3 verbs onto ce-rs put_object/get_object (1 MiB client-side chunking, content-addressed, hash-verified); src/range.rs is pure, well-tested ranged-read math; src/caps.rs mints/verifies ce-cap chains as presigned-equivalent links; src/gateway.rs is an axum S3-subset HTTP server (feature-gated); src/main.rs is the CLI. The core verbs genuinely work end-to-end (live.rs/gateway_live.rs exercise put/get/ranged-get/list/delete/copy/dedup against ephemeral nodes). There are no todo!()/unimplemented!()/panic! in production paths; unwrap()/expect() appear only in tests. The honest gaps are stated in the README: no versioning, no multipart upload, no server-side encryption, no SigV4 auth on the gateway, no lifecycle, and the gateway/CLI never actually call verify_link (capability enforcement is a library function nobody wires in). It is a single-writer, single-node design with no concurrency control across processes.
plan: 1. Add a DefaultBodyLimit to the gateway router and a configurable max-object-size to Store::put_object; reject oversized uploads with 413 instead of OOM. This closes the most severe DoS.

2. Wire capability enforcement into the gateway: add a middleware that extracts a ce-cap token (header or query param) and calls caps::verify_link for the bucket/key/ability before each handler runs; return 403 on failure. Add an e2e test proving unauthorized requests are rejected. Without this, presign is theater.

3. Stream objects instead of buffering: change put_object/get_object to accept/return an AsyncRead/Stream (or chunk-at-a-time API) so large objects and ranged reads never materialize the whole body; update the gateway to stream request/response bodies.

4. Make the index concurrency-safe: add an advisory file lock (e.g. fs2/flock) around load+mutate+save, or move to an append-only log / embedded KV (redb/sled) so concurrent CLI+gateway processes cannot lose writes. Add a concurrency test.

5. Implement multipart/resumable upload (CreateMultipartUpload/UploadPart/CompleteMultipartUpload/Abort) backed by the existing chunk/manifest model — parts map naturally to blob chunks; assemble a manifest on complete.

6. Add object versioning: make a bucket optionally versioned, store a version list per key with version-ids (the CID is a perfect version id), support GetObject?versionId and delete markers, and keep last-N or all noncurrent versions.

7. Add user metadata + standard headers: extend ObjectMeta with a metadata: BTreeMap<String,String> plus cache_control/content_disposition/content_encoding; surface them on PutObject/HeadObject/GetObject in the gateway (x-amz-meta-*).
8. Fix the property-test/impl contradiction: change prop_storage.rs scope invariant to the boundary-aware rule and add boundary cases (prefix='a' vs key='ab') so the property matches scope_allows.
9. Implement conditional requests (If-Match/If-None-Match/If-Modified-Since -> 304/412) using the CID ETag, enabling cache validation and optimistic-concurrency writes.

10. Improve ListObjectsV2 fidelity: emit LastModified, KeyCount, MaxKeys, StartAfter, and seek into the BTreeMap via range(start_after..) instead of skip-scanning; make continuation tokens opaque/url-safe; add encoding-type support.
11. Correct gateway error mapping: distinguish 404 (NoSuchKey/NoSuchBucket) from 5xx (node/IO failure) and return 416 for unsatisfiable ranges; propagate real causes.
12. Wire durability: actually call ce-pin on PutObject (configurable replication factor) and unpin/GC on DeleteObject so DeleteObject reclaims bytes; remove or implement the pin_hint stub.
13. Implement lifecycle rules (expiration TTL, optional storage-class transition) and bulk DeleteObjects; add a background sweeper.
14. Implement the advertised sealed-object (client-side encryption-before-store) mode and the index snapshot_cid blob mirroring, or remove those claims from the docs.
15. Convert the README library example into a compiled doctest, add an examples/ presign->verify_link demo, and write ARCHITECTURE.md documenting the single-writer model and operational limits.

featureGaps.feature: Object versioning (S3 versioning / GCS generations, version-id, delete markers)
featureGaps.notes: ObjectMeta stores one version per key; PutObject overwrites in place. No version-id, no GetObject?versionId, no noncurrent-version retention. README admits this is out of scope.

featureGaps.feature: Multipart / resumable upload (CreateMultipartUpload, UploadPart, CompleteMultipartUpload, AbortMultipartUpload)
featureGaps.notes: Both put_object (Store) and the gateway buffer the ENTIRE object in memory as Vec<u8>/Bytes. No streaming, no part-level upload, so large objects are impossible and a DoS via a single huge PUT is trivial.

featureGaps.feature: Server-side encryption at rest (SSE-S3 / SSE-KMS / SSE-C; the 'sealed objects' idea in lib.rs)
featureGaps.notes: Bytes are stored plaintext content-addressed; the host can read everything. lib.rs advertises encrypt-before-store as a 'natural extension' but nothing implements it.

featureGaps.feature: Gateway authentication (AWS SigV4) and any auth at all on the HTTP gateway
featureGaps.notes: gateway.rs has zero auth; anyone who can reach the bind address can read/write/delete every bucket. verify_link in caps.rs is never called by the gateway or CLI, so presigned links are minted but never enforced.
No lifecycle rules, no automatic expiry, no storage classes. DeleteObject does not even unpin/GC bytes (pin_hint is a stub handoff that nothing consumes).
ObjectMeta carries only content_type. No arbitrary user metadata map, no standard caching/disposition headers stored or served.
No conditional GET/PUT; the strong ETag (CID) exists but is never used for 304/412 semantics or optimistic concurrency.

featureGaps.feature: Bucket policy / ACL / IAM, and per-bucket public/private state
featureGaps.notes: No access policy at the bucket level; authorization is only the unused capability links.
Only single-key delete; S3 DeleteObjects multi-object op missing. Delete is idempotent (correct) but gateway maps delete errors to NoSuchBucket only.

featureGaps.feature: ListObjectsV2 fidelity: returns owner, LastModified, full marker/KeyCount/MaxKeys/StartAfter/EncodingType fields, urlencoding
featureGaps.notes: list XML omits LastModified, Owner, KeyCount, MaxKeys, encoding; continuation token is a raw key (works but not opaque/url-safe).

featureGaps.feature: CopyObject across the HTTP gateway (x-amz-copy-source) and server-side metadata-replace copy
featureGaps.notes: CopyObject exists in the library/CLI but is not exposed via the gateway PUT+x-amz-copy-source header.
Durability is documented as a handoff to ce-pin via pin_hint(), but nothing calls it; bytes live only on the local node until manually pinned.

robustnessIssues.issue: Whole-object buffering: Store::put_object takes &[u8], get_object returns Vec<u8>, and the gateway uses axum Bytes body + returns full Vec. A single large PUT/GET loads the entire object into RAM with no size limit (DoS / OOM). No streaming, no max-body limit, no DefaultBodyLimit on the axum router.

Capability links are minted but never enforced. The gateway performs no auth and never calls caps::verify_link; verify_link/scope_allows are dead code from the running app's perspective, so 'presigned access' provides no real protection.
robustnessIssues.location: src/gateway.rs (all handlers), src/caps.rs:134 verify_link unused outside tests

robustnessIssues.issue: No cross-process / concurrent-writer safety on the index file. Two Store instances (e.g. CLI invocation while the gateway runs) each load, mutate, and atomically rename buckets.json, silently clobbering each other's writes (last-writer-wins, lost updates). The gateway serializes via a Mutex but any second process bypasses it.

get_object_range falls back to fetching the WHOLE object then slicing when the manifest can't be parsed. Combined with unbounded object size this defeats the point of ranged reads for the inline-blob path and can pull a huge object for a tiny range.

valid_key/valid_bucket_name are enforced on put/make_bucket but NOT on the read/list/head/delete paths or in scope/caveat parsing. A key with embedded '/' patterns or a gateway path can be listed/deleted without validation; scope path_prefix containing the bucket delimiter is parsed by split_once('/') and is ambiguous if a bucket name ever contained '/' (S3 names can't, but no invariant enforces it here).

Gateway swallows/parse-skips invalid header values silently: get_object/head_object insert headers only `if let Ok(val) = v.parse()`, so a non-ASCII content_type or oversized ETag silently drops the header rather than erroring, producing a response that misreports content type/length.

ListObjectsV2 delimiter rollup uses common_prefixes.contains(&cp) inside the per-key loop — O(n*m) on buckets with many distinct folders; with large buckets this is quadratic. Also the whole index is held in memory and walked linearly per list call (no range seek into the BTreeMap from start_after).

now() and the gateway error paths map almost everything to NoSuchKey/NoSuchBucket regardless of actual cause (e.g. a network/IO failure from the node becomes a 404 NoSuchKey), masking real 5xx errors from clients.

Index is loaded fully into memory and persisted as pretty JSON on every single mutation (flush per put/delete/copy/mb/rb). For a bucket with millions of keys, each write rewrites the entire JSON file — O(total) write amplification per object op.
mint_link uses nonce = now() (seconds) in the CLI; two presign calls within the same second collide on nonce, so on-chain revoke-by-(issuer,nonce) cannot distinguish them.

testGaps.area: Property test asserts scope_allows == raw starts_with (prop_storage.rs:177-179), contradicting the boundary-aware scope_allows impl (caps.rs:68-76). e.g. prefix='a', key='ab' makes starts_with=true but scope_allows=false; generators ([a-z0-9/]) can produce this — a latent failing/incorrect property invariant that should be tightened to the boundary rule.
Concurrent writers to the same index file (two Store processes), and concurrent gateway requests racing put/delete on the same key — no test exercises lost-update or interleaving.
Large-object / memory-pressure behavior (objects > available RAM, ranged read on multi-GB object) and absence of body-size limits.
Capability enforcement wired into a request path: there is no e2e test of an unauthorized request being rejected, because nothing enforces caps; verify_link is only unit-tested in isolation.

Gateway failure paths: malformed Range -> 416 (gateway never returns 416; parse error becomes a 404), oversized body, missing content-type, list with bad max-keys, XML escaping of keys containing quotes/control chars.
Index corruption/partial-write recovery (crash between temp write and rename), and migration/forward-compat of the JSON schema.
snapshot_cid / index mirroring into the blob store is described in index.rs module docs but neither implemented nor tested.

docGaps: No CHANGELOG, no ARCHITECTURE.md; the design-vs-reality boundary (what is stubbed) lives only in scattered README/rustdoc prose.

README/lib.rs advertise 'sealed (encrypt-before-store) objects', ce-pin durability handoff, and an index snapshot_cid that are aspirational and not implemented — readers may assume they work. Mark clearly as TODO.
No rustdoc doctests; the README library example is not compiled as a doctest, so it can rot.
No examples/ directory despite Cargo.toml categories implying CLI/network usage; a runnable example wiring presign->verify_link end-to-end would document the (currently missing) auth flow.
Gateway security posture ('run on a trusted host / front with a cap check') is stated but there is no documented, supported way to actually turn on the cap check — the enforcement hook is absent.
No documentation of the index file format, its concurrency model (single-writer assumption), or operational limits (max object size, max keys/bucket).

ce-iam.name: ce-iam
ce-iam.currentState: ce-iam is a Rust (edition 2024) library + CLI that maps AWS-IAM vocabulary (principals, roles, policies, mint/attenuate/verify/inspect/revoke) onto the ce-cap signed attenuating capability primitive. The security core is genuinely real and complete: Iam::mint, attenuate (with pre-sign amplification checks for abilities/resource/caveats), verify/verify_chain (delegating to ce_cap::authorize), inspect, decode, plus wildcard expansion against a closed action universe at mint time. Policy/Statement/Effect/ResourceMatch/Conditions compile faithfully to ce_cap Resource/Caveats; Effect::Deny deserializes but is correctly rejected at compile time. The Catalog (roles/policies/attachments/audit/effective_grants) is implemented as a deterministic op-log state machine, but it is only a *model* of ce-coord: the doc comments admit the live wiring onto ce_coord::RMap (persistence, networking, replication) does not exist — there is no on-disk store, no node, no actual replication, and the CLI exposes none of the catalog. Revocation is a fetch-only HTTP snapshot (RevocationSet::fetch via ce-rs) plus a direct POST /capabilities/revoke for self-issued nonces. No unwrap/expect/panic/todo!/unimplemented! exist in non-test paths (verified by grep); all unwraps are in #[cfg(test)] or examples. Testing is unusually strong for a young crate: per-function unit tests, 8 property tests (attenuation-never-amplifies, expiry, revocation, wrong-issuer/audience, malformed-never-panics, round-trip), failure-injection against dead nodes, and catalog convergence/safety property tests. The CLI (whoami/grant/verify/revoke/policy new|validate|inspect) is a thin, mostly-offline wrapper but is missing the catalog, wallet, and several condition flags.
plan: Wire the Catalog to real durability/replication: implement an on-disk persisted catalog store (e.g. <data_dir>/iam/catalog.json with atomic write) and the actual ce_coord::RMap writer/reader path the doc comments promise, with a reload/round-trip integration test; update docs to stop implying durability that does not exist.

Add catalog/role CLI subcommands: `ce-iam role put|get|list|rm`, `ce-iam attach|detach <principal> <role>`, `ce-iam effective-grants <principal>`, `ce-iam audit [--since v]`, and `ce-iam grant --role <name>` (mint_role). Wire them to the persisted store from step 1.

Implement a wallet: a WalletStore library type + `ce-iam wallet add|list|show|rm` backed by <data_dir>/iam/wallet.json, so minted/received tokens can be stored, labeled, inspected, and presented; integrate with `verify` so a stored token can be referenced by label.

Harden verify against untrusted input: enforce a configurable max chain depth and max token byte size before decoding/authorizing, and add property + failure-path tests for oversized/deep tokens (DoS bound).
Fix expires_in overflow (saturating_add in build_conditions) and add a regression test; audit all arithmetic on time/limits for wrapping.

Add revocation freshness/operational policy: last-known-good snapshot caching with a TTL, an explicit --fail-closed flag (deny when the revoke set is unfetchable) vs the current implicit fail-open, and a `ce-iam revoked list/inspect` command; test stale and unreachable cases.

Expose the full Conditions surface in the CLI: --not-before/--activates-in, --allowed-port (repeatable), --path-prefix; add Iam::verify-level tests proving each caveat (not_before, ports, path_prefix, max_cpu/mem/credits) actually gates a request through ce-iam, not just ce-cap.

Add root-rotation support: commands/library helpers to add and retire accepted roots with overlapping validity, a re-issuance helper to migrate live grants to a new root, and tests covering verification across a rotation window.

Add CLI e2e tests (assert_cmd or trycmd): grant/verify exit codes (0 ALLOW / 1 DENY), --json shapes, policy validate from file and stdin, and the full mint->attenuate->verify->revoke flow against the binary.

Support multi-scope policies properly: instead of rejecting Allow statements with differing resource/conditions, provide Iam::mint_policy that returns the N grants a multi-scope document implies, and document the one-capability-per-scope rule; add tests.
Bound catalog growth: cap/rotate the audit log (or make it pageable/persisted separately) and add optional limits on roles/policies/attachments to remove the unbounded-memory path.

Documentation pass: disclose stub status, add a Google-IAM comparison section, document not_before/ports/path_prefix, add SECURITY.md (verifier trust model + revocation operation guidance), a CHANGELOG, and doctests for the new CLI/catalog/wallet APIs.

featureGaps.notes: CLAUDE.md and the capability model reference a wallet (`ce wallet add ... --cap <token>`), but ce-iam has no command or type to persist, list, label, or look up grants the principal holds. Today a token is printed to stdout and the user must paste it manually. Needs a `ce-iam wallet add/list/show/rm` backed by a file store (e.g. <data_dir>/iam/wallet.json) plus a library WalletStore type.

featureGaps.feature: Catalog is not actually wired to ce-coord (no persistence/replication)
featureGaps.notes: catalog.rs is a deterministic StateMachine model with a CatalogLog, but no real ce_coord::RMap integration, no on-disk persistence, and no mesh broadcast — the doc comment explicitly says the live wiring 'maps it onto ce_coord::Version' but that code is absent. So the managed role/policy/attachment store and audit log only exist in-process for tests. Implement actual ce-coord wiring or at minimum a persisted single-node catalog store.

featureGaps.feature: No CLI surface for catalog / roles / effective grants / mint_role
featureGaps.notes: Iam::mint_role, Catalog CRUD, attach/detach, effective_grants, and audit are library-only. A real IAM product needs `ce-iam role put/get/list/rm`, `attach/detach`, `effective-grants <principal>`, `audit`. None exist in src/main.rs.

Iam::with_accepted_roots exists but there is no rotation workflow: no command to add/retire a root, no notion of overlapping validity windows, no re-issuance helper when a root key rotates. The task reference set lists root rotation as a required capability.

featureGaps.feature: not_before caveat is unreachable from the CLI
featureGaps.notes: Conditions has not_before and ce-cap enforces it, but GrantArgs/PolicyNewArgs expose only --expires-in. There is no --not-before / --activates-in flag, so time-windowed (future-dated) grants cannot be minted via the CLI.

featureGaps.feature: Port/path/credit caveats unreachable or partially reachable from CLI
featureGaps.notes: Conditions supports allowed_ports and path_prefix (both enforced by ce-cap), but the CLI grant command exposes neither; --max-cpu/--max-mem-mb/--max-credits are exposed but allowed_ports/path_prefix are not. Tunnel/sync grants therefore cannot be scoped from the CLI.

featureGaps.feature: No condition/context keys beyond time and resource ceilings
featureGaps.notes: AWS IAM conditions include source IP, MFA, request tags, string/date/numeric operators. ce-iam only models expiry + a few quantity ceilings + node tags. This is partly inherent to offline capabilities, but documenting the gap and supporting a small caveat-key extension surface would help.

featureGaps.feature: Service-control / boundary policies and deny semantics
featureGaps.notes: By design capabilities are monotone grants, so Deny / permission boundaries / SCPs are intentionally unsupported. This is a reasonable model choice but is a real functional gap vs IAM; the docs cover it well.
Principal is a single node id; there is no group abstraction (attach a role to a group). Roles are attached per-principal in the catalog. A group layer would reduce per-principal churn.

featureGaps.feature: Revocation reachable only for self-issued nonces; no delegated revocation UX
featureGaps.notes: submit_revoke targets the local node's own key; there is no flow to surface or manage revocation of intermediate links in a multi-party chain, nor a `list-revoked`/inspect-revoked command.

robustnessIssues.issue: Potential u64 overflow computing expiry: `now_secs() + expires_in` can wrap if a caller passes a huge --expires-in, silently producing a small/garbage not_after. Use saturating_add.

RevocationSet::fetch has no caching or fail-closed/fail-open policy: cmd_verify bails with an error if the node is unreachable, forcing the operator to choose --no-revocation-check (fail-open). There is no last-known-good snapshot, TTL, or explicit fail-closed mode, so a transient node outage either blocks all verification or silently drops revocation enforcement.

No bound on capability chain depth at decode/verify time within ce-iam. inspect_chain/decode/verify accept arbitrarily long decoded chains; a maliciously large token could cause large allocations / slow verify. ce-cap may bound this, but ce-iam should enforce a max-depth/max-token-size limit before processing untrusted tokens (DoS surface for any service calling verify on remote-supplied tokens).

effective_grants sort comparator falls back to unwrap_or_default() on serde_json::to_string failure, so the 'deterministic order' guarantee is technically not airtight if scope serialization ever fails.

compile() flattens multi-statement Allow policies but requires identical resource AND conditions across all Allow statements, rejecting otherwise-valid multi-scope IAM documents with a generic BadPolicy. There is no helper that mints the N grants a multi-scope policy implies, so the model silently constrains expressiveness.

Catalog has no size/quantity limits: unbounded roles, policies, attachments, and an ever-growing in-memory audit Vec with no rotation/truncation — an unbounded-memory growth path for a long-lived catalog.
submit_revoke uses resp.text().await.unwrap_or_default() on the error path — no panic, but silently discards the body-read error, masking why a revoke was rejected.

policy validate writes a throwaway identity key to a predictable temp dir (ce-iam-scratch-<pid>) just to compile-check a policy; reused across runs of the same pid and world-readable temp. An in-memory identity would avoid writing a key to disk.

testGaps.area: Caveat enforcement at the IAM verify layer (max_cpu/max_mem_mb/max_credits/allowed_ports/path_prefix) — ce-cap tests these, but ce-iam has no test that a Conditions-built grant actually denies an over-limit request through Iam::verify
not_before / future-dated grants honored through Iam::verify (only not_after/expiry is property-tested; not_before is enforced by ce-cap but never exercised via ce-iam)
Multi-link (depth >= 3) attenuation property tests for resources and caveats narrowing (the depth-3 e2e test exists but property tests only build depth-2 chains)
expires_in overflow / saturating behavior in build_conditions
CLI integration/e2e tests: no test invokes the ce-iam binary (grant/verify exit codes, --json output, policy validate from file/stdin); cmd_* functions are entirely untested
Real ce-coord catalog replication and persistence round-trip (current tests exercise the in-process model only; there is no test against an actual ce_coord::RMap or a persisted reload)
Concurrent catalog writers / conflicting ops ordering (single-writer is assumed but never tested under interleaving; replay_order_independence only covers disjoint keys)
Large/malformed token DoS bounds (huge chains, deeply nested) — malformed-never-panics is tested up to 256 random bytes only, not adversarially large valid-ish chains
RevocationSet staleness/caching semantics and fail-closed mode (only dead-node graceful-Err is tested)

docGaps: README/lib docs call the catalog a 'managed product' and describe it as ce-coord-backed but do not disclose that the live ce-coord wiring/persistence is unimplemented — a reader would reasonably believe roles/audit are durable and replicated. State the stub status explicitly.

The crate is documented as AWS-IAM-shaped; the audit framed it as Google IAM. No doc maps the model to Google IAM concepts (roles/bindings/conditions/service accounts) or explains divergences. A short comparison section would orient GCP users.
No rustdoc/doctest or CLI docs for the catalog, mint_role, or effective_grants because they are library-only with no CLI; the README CLI section omits them entirely.
No architecture/ADR doc explaining the CE-vs-app boundary for IAM, the wallet relationship to `ce wallet`, or how revocation freshness should be operated (TTL, fail-open vs fail-closed).

not_before, allowed_ports, and path_prefix conditions are implemented in the library but undocumented and unreachable from the CLI; README condition syntax lists only expires-in/max-cpu/max-mem-mb/max-credits.
No CHANGELOG and no SECURITY.md describing the verifier trust model and what a verifier must do (accept-roots configuration, revocation freshness) to be safe.

ce-pubsub.name: ce-pubsub
ce-pubsub.currentState: ce-pubsub is a small (~1050 LOC src), clean, genuinely-working library + CLI that composes Google-Pub/Sub-like semantics from CE primitives: live fan-out via a ce-coord typed Stream over mesh gossip (at-most-once), and a durable single-writer append log (TopicLog, a ce-coord StateMachine with Snapshot) for at-least-once cursor-based replay. The architecture is real, not stubbed: src/log.rs implements append/prune/since/cursor algebra with absolute cursors surviving prunes; src/lib.rs wires create_topic (writer + live stream + ingest worker), publish, subscribe, pull (read replica + convergence wait), and remote publish_to over ce-rs directed request/reply; src/caps.rs mints/verifies attenuating ce-cap publish/subscribe tokens scoped by topic prefix. The underlying ce-coord (writer/snapshot_reader/checkpoint/Stream) and ce-rs (request/reply/messages/revoked/reply_token) APIs all exist and are used correctly. No todo!/unimplemented!/panic in production paths; every unwrap/expect is confined to tests. The honest gaps are conceptual feature breadth (no ack/nack, no dead-letter, no flow control, no push, no ordering keys, no subscription model) and a few robustness holes (no payload-size limit, heuristic convergence that can silently under-replay, single-writer SPOF, de-dup set that resets). It is a strong v1 of the publish/replay core, not a full Pub/Sub.
plan: Fix the writer-lifecycle hazard: make create_topic idempotent / guard against multiple concurrent writers per topic, and change main.rs Publish --own to attach to an existing owner (directed publish_to self or a long-lived owner handle) instead of opening a new Replicated writer + ingest loop on every invocation. Give serve_ingest a cancellation handle tied to Topic drop.

Add hard bounds to prevent DoS: a configurable max message size (reject in publish/handle_ingest before append) and a max in-flight pull batch (max_messages/max_bytes on pull, returning a continuation cursor). Document the defaults.

Make pull() convergence explicit and honest: replace the timing heuristic with a target-version wait (ask the owner for its current high_cursor via a directed request, then converge the replica until it reaches that version or return a typed NotConverged error) so an incomplete replay is never silently returned as complete.

Introduce a Subscription resource with server-tracked ack state: a durable per-subscription cursor in a ce-coord log keyed by (topic, subscriber), plus ack(cursor)/nack and an ack-deadline lease, so at-least-once is server-enforced rather than client-cursor bookkeeping. This is the single biggest step toward real Pub/Sub semantics.

Add a dead-letter policy: track delivery attempts per message-per-subscription and, on exceeding max_delivery_attempts, route the message to a configured dead-letter topic; expose dead_letter_policy on subscription creation.

Harden authorization: stop recording an unauthenticated publisher on open topics (either authenticate m.from via ce-cap/identity always, or rename the field to claimed_publisher and update docs); make fetch_revoked fail-closed (or surface revocation-unavailable as an error for cap-gated publishes); populate accepted_roots from config so the multi-org cap story actually works.

Add idempotent remote publish: let publish_to carry a client idempotency key the owner dedups on across the handled-set reset, replacing the wholesale handled.clear() with a bounded time/LRU eviction, so a retried publish after a lost reply does not double-append.

Add ordering keys and per-message attributes: extend Message with an attributes map and an optional ordering_key, and provide a filter expression on subscribe/pull, matching Google's attribute filtering and per-key FIFO.
Add automatic retention: a message_retention_duration policy on the topic that the owner enforces by periodic prune_to/checkpoint, plus seek-by-timestamp on pull using published_at.
Add push subscriptions: a delivery worker that POSTs new messages to a configured endpoint with retry/backoff and ack-on-2xx, reusing the subscription ack-cursor machinery.

Close the test gaps: add a compiled examples/ end-to-end demo, CLI-level e2e tests, concurrency tests for cursor assignment under concurrent appends, de-dup-across-reset tests, multi-hop capability attenuation tests, and ensure the live mesh tests run in CI (build the ce binary in the workflow rather than skipping when absent).

featureGaps.feature: Subscription as a first-class durable resource with per-subscription ack state
featureGaps.notes: Google Pub/Sub decouples topic from subscription: each subscription has its own independently-advanced acked cursor and backlog. Here a 'subscription' is just a live stream or a stateless pull(from); the server tracks no per-subscriber position, so the client must persist its own cursor. No createSubscription/deleteSubscription/listSubscriptions surface.

There is no acknowledgement protocol. pull() returns messages but the owner never learns they were processed; redelivery/leasing/ack-deadline (the core of at-least-once with backpressure) does not exist. A consumer crash mid-processing has no server-side redelivery — it just re-pulls from a cursor it chooses.

featureGaps.feature: Dead-letter topics and max delivery attempts
featureGaps.notes: No dead_letter_policy, no delivery-attempt counter, no max_delivery_attempts. Poison messages cannot be routed aside; they would be replayed forever by any puller starting before them.

Google Pub/Sub supports ordering_key for per-key FIFO. Here ordering is global per topic by single-writer cursor (stronger than needed) but there is no per-key partitioning, and remote publish_to has no ordering guarantee relative to other publishers since ingest is polled every 250ms and de-duped by reply_token, not sequenced by publisher.

featureGaps.feature: Flow control / backpressure (max outstanding messages or bytes)
featureGaps.notes: subscribe()'s live ring is bounded by the node inbox and silently drops; pull() returns the entire tail unbounded in one Vec with no max_messages/max_bytes limit — a large backlog is materialized fully in memory.
Only pull (live tail + durable pull) exists. No push subscription that POSTs to an endpoint with retry/backoff, which is half of Google Pub/Sub's delivery model.

featureGaps.feature: Seek / replay by timestamp and retention-based seek
featureGaps.notes: Replay is by integer cursor only. Messages carry published_at, but there is no seek(time) to replay from a wall-clock point, and no snapshot/seek-to-snapshot resource as Google offers.

featureGaps.feature: Configurable message retention duration + retain-acked option
featureGaps.notes: Retention is fully manual: the owner must call prune_to/checkpoint by hand. No message_retention_duration policy, no automatic time- or size-based pruning, no per-subscription retain-acked-messages.
Message is just cursor+publisher+payload+published_at. No key/value attributes map and no subscription-side filter expression (Google's filter on attributes).
Payloads are opaque bytes; no schema association or publish-time validation.
Google offers exactly-once within a subscription. Here de-dup is best-effort over a HashSet that is cleared at 16384 entries, so cross-reset duplicates are possible; no server-dedup token.
README admits single-writer SPOF: if the owner node is offline, publishing stalls and no failover exists. Google's brokered topics have no single owner node.

robustnessIssues.issue: No payload-size limit on publish or ingest. Topic::append_and_fanout and handle_ingest accept arbitrary-length bytes; the durable TopicLog holds every message in an in-memory Vec<Message> with no per-message or per-topic cap. A single large remote publish (hex payload over the mesh) or a flood of messages is an unbounded-memory DoS on the owner.

await_convergence is a timing heuristic (300ms quiet-window, 8s hard deadline) that returns the replica's current state with NO error if it has not actually converged. A slow/large log or a laggy replica yields a silently-incomplete replay, breaking the documented at-least-once contract; the caller cannot distinguish 'no more messages' from 'gave up early'.

Ingest de-dup set is cleared wholesale at 16384 entries (handled.clear()). After a clear, a request re-delivered from the inbox ring whose reply_token was already processed will be appended a second time (duplicate publish under load), and there is no time-based eviction, so legitimate distinct tokens can be dropped from de-dup mid-stream.

The ingest worker spawns a detached infinite tokio task (serve_ingest) with a 250ms poll loop that is never cancelled or joined; dropping the Topic does not stop it. Multiple create_topic calls for the same topic (e.g. the `--own` publish path calls create_topic each invocation) each spawn their own writer+ingest loop, racing as concurrent single-writers on the same ce-coord log.
robustnessIssues.location: src/lib.rs:335-371 (serve_ingest), src/main.rs:175 (--own re-creates the topic per publish)

robustnessIssues.issue: PruneTo retention floor accounting trusts that pruned messages were a contiguous prefix; LogOp::PruneTo uses retain(|m| m.cursor > up_to) and increments floor by the count removed. If the log ever held a non-contiguous cursor sequence (it should not, but there is no invariant check on Append that cursor == next_cursor), floor would silently desync from absolute positions. Append does not validate the stamped cursor matches next_cursor.
robustnessIssues.location: src/log.rs:43-52 (apply), no validation in LogOp::Append handling

robustnessIssues.issue: Remote publisher identity in handle_ingest is taken from m.from (the transport-reported sender) and recorded verbatim as the authenticated publisher, but when require_cap is false there is NO verification that m.from is genuine — a message's recorded 'publisher' is whatever the transport claims. Open topics record an unauthenticated publisher field that subscribers are told (in Message docs) they 'can trust without re-checking'.
robustnessIssues.location: src/lib.rs:355 (handle_ingest call), src/message.rs:25-26 (publisher doc claims authenticated)

robustnessIssues.issue: fetch_revoked swallows all errors and treats the revocation set as empty on any failure (Err(_) => Vec::new()). A node API outage means revoked capabilities are silently honored as valid for publish — fail-open on revocation.

live fan-out failure is only logged (tracing::warn) and the publish still returns Ok(cursor); a subscriber relying solely on live delivery gets no signal that fan-out dropped, and the owner has already committed durably — acceptable by design but undocumented as a partial-success.

publish_to has no retry/idempotency: a timeout waiting for the owner's reply returns an error, but the owner may have already appended (the reply was lost). Re-publishing then double-appends since reply_token differs on the retry. No client-supplied idempotency key.

accepted_roots is always empty (Vec::new() at construction, never populated) and there is no API to add cross-org roots, so cap chains can only ever root at the owner's own key despite verify_link plumbing accepted_roots through. Dead capability path.

testGaps.area: Concurrent publishers racing the single-writer log (interleaved next_cursor reads under propose) — no test that two append_and_fanout calls assign distinct contiguous cursors
Ingest de-dup correctness across the handled.clear() reset boundary (duplicate suppression after 16384 tokens)
pull() against a non-converged / slow replica returning incomplete results (await_convergence early-deadline behavior is untested; live test only asserts the happy 6-message convergence)
publish_to timeout / lost-reply / owner-offline paths (only the cap-gating live test exercises publish_to, all with a healthy owner)
Capability re-delegation / attenuation chains (multi-link narrowing of topic prefix) — minted chains in tests are always single self-issued links, never multi-hop
Large-payload / large-backlog behavior and any size limit (none exists, none tested)
End-to-end through the CLI binary (no test invokes main.rs verbs; the --own create_topic-per-publish double-writer hazard is untested)

Live tests are skip-by-default (require a prebuilt ce binary at a hardcoded path) so CI almost certainly never runs the mesh paths — fan-out, durable replica convergence, and cap gating are effectively unverified in automation
Append cursor-invariant violation (a stamped cursor != next_cursor) and its effect on floor/since

docGaps: README and lib.rs assert delivery guarantees ('at-least-once', publisher 'authenticated', 'subscribers can trust it without re-checking') that the open-topic code path does not actually enforce — the authentication claim is misleading for non-cap-gated topics.
No documentation of the absence of ack/nack, dead-letter, ordering keys, flow control, or push — a reader comparing to Google Pub/Sub gets no explicit 'not supported' list (only scaling caveats).
await_convergence's best-effort/heuristic nature and its silent-incomplete-replay failure mode are not documented as a caveat on pull().
No payload size limit is documented; users will assume one exists.
No examples/ directory and no runnable example binary; the only sample is the README/lib doctest (marked no_run), so there is no compiled, executed end-to-end example.
main.rs --own path creating a fresh writer per publish (and its concurrency implication vs a long-running create-topic owner) is undocumented.

No architecture/design doc (e.g. docs/) explaining the two-transport (Coord + separate CeClient) model, the single-writer ownership model, or the intended retention workflow beyond README scaling notes.
Capability accepted_roots / cross-org root configuration is plumbed but undocumented and unreachable, so the multi-org authorization story in caps.rs rustdoc overstates current capability.

ce-db.name: ce-db
ce-db.currentState: ce-db is a clean, app-tier Rust SDK (edition 2024) that reimagines Firestore by composing CE primitives: ce-coord `Merged` (a multi-writer CRDT union-of-logs) for the document store, ce-cap for capability gating, and ce-coord `Snapshot` for compaction. The architecture is genuinely sound and the pure model works: `DbMachine` (src/doc.rs) is a deterministic, order-independent field-level LWW merge machine with whole-doc Set, field-level Patch (null = field delete), and Delete tombstones with resurrection; the query engine (src/query.rs) does conjunctive top-level field filters + order + limit; the access layer (src/access.rs) wraps ce-cap minting/attenuation/verification. Convergence, query semantics, capability attenuation, and serde of large numbers are all proven by strong unit + proptest + edge-case suites, plus a real two-node ephemeral-mesh live integration suite (tests/live.rs, tests/realtime_sync.rs) that gracefully skips when the ce binary is absent. No `todo!()`/`unimplemented!()`/`panic!` in production paths; the only `println!` are in the CLI binary (allowed). The big honesty gap: the capability layer is a self-contained module never wired into `Collection` read/write paths (no enforcement point), reads do a full O(n) re-fold of the entire op union on every call with no materialized cache, and "realtime watch" actually requires an external poll loop because `watch` only fires when something calls `pull()`.
plan: Wire capability enforcement into the data path: add a guarded Collection (or an auth policy passed to open) that calls CollectionGrant::verify for db:read on get/query/watch and db:write on set/patch/delete, against configured accepted roots and an on-chain is_revoked closure. Add integration tests proving unauthorized reads/writes are denied. This closes the single largest credibility gap vs Firestore.

Add a materialized document cache inside Collection invalidated by the watch counter, so get/query/len are not full O(n) re-folds of the whole op history on every call. Benchmark before/after on a 10k-op collection.

Make realtime push-based: have ce-coord (or a Collection-owned background task) call pull() and fire the watch when peer ops arrive, so next_change delivers peer changes without an external poller. Then add an e2e test that asserts a peer write wakes a watcher with no manual refresh.

Implement per-document change deltas (added/modified/removed, i.e. Firestore DocumentChange) and a query-scoped/doc-scoped listener API on top of snapshots, so onSnapshot semantics are real, not whole-collection diffs.
Add query cursors (startAt/startAfter/endAt/endBefore + offset) and multi-field order_by with doc-id tie-break, enabling real pagination over ordered results.

Add input validation and DoS limits: max document byte size, max field count, max collection op-count/peer-count, validated collection/doc-id charset and length, returning anyhow errors on violation. Add failure-path tests.
Implement subcollections: hierarchical addressing (collection/doc/sub/doc), per-subcollection Merged instances, and collection-group queries. This is core Firestore data modeling.

Add transactions and batched writes: a WriteBatch that proposes multiple DocOps atomically into the writer log, and a runTransaction optimistic read-then-write helper (documenting the CRDT caveat that cross-device atomicity still needs the chain).

Fix integer-precision in ordering/comparison (compare i64/u64 numerically instead of routing through as_f64), and harden the Lamport allocator to a single critical section / parking_lot mutex to remove lock().unwrap() and the two-lock race.
Add richer operators (array-contains-any, in, not-in) and nested field-path support for both queries and Patch field-merge (dotted paths), so nested objects merge field-by-field like Firestore.
Add FieldValue-style atomic ops (serverTimestamp, increment, arrayUnion/arrayRemove) as new DocOp kinds with CRDT-friendly merge semantics (e.g. a PN-counter for increment).
Improve compaction: GC tombstones and superseded patch ops across the merged set (not just the local writer log), and add a snapshot+tail == full-replay equivalence test at the ce-db level.

Documentation pass: add examples/, correct the README claims about enforcement/realtime, fix the stale tests/ layout list, add an architecture doc covering the cost model and CRDT-vs-consensus boundary, and document supported query shapes and limits.

featureGaps.notes: CollectionGrant::verify exists and is well-tested but Collection::set/patch/delete/get/query never call it. There is no enforcement point: any holder of a Coord can read/write any collection. Firestore security rules actually gate every operation. Needs a guarded Collection wrapper that verifies db:read/db:write/db:admin per op against accepted roots + on-chain revocation.

Firestore's core hierarchical model (collection/doc/subcollection/doc...) is absent. DocPath keeps a slash-containing doc_id but there is no subcollection addressing, no collection-group queries, no nested document tree. Only flat collections exist.
No startAt/startAfter/endAt/endBefore/offset. Only limit (truncate). Firestore pagination over ordered results is impossible; a UI cannot page through large collections.

No runTransaction (read-then-conditional-write with optimistic concurrency) and no WriteBatch (atomic multi-doc commit). README correctly punts uniqueness/balances to the chain, but Firestore-style transactional multi-doc commits are a headline feature that is entirely missing.

watch/next_change only deliver a whole-collection Snapshot on any change. There is no per-document added/modified/removed delta stream (Firestore's DocumentChange), no doc-scoped listener, no query-scoped listener. A UI must diff full snapshots itself.

Every query is a full O(n) scan of the folded map (query.rs run). No single-field or composite indexes, no index-backed range scans. README admits this but Firestore's defining performance trait (indexed queries) is absent, so large collections are linear.

Missing array-contains-any, in, not-in, !=-with-index, and OR queries. Only Eq/Ne/Gt/Ge/Lt/Le/Contains on AND-ed top-level fields. order_by supports a single field only (no multi-field sort, no order on missing-tie-break by doc id).

Filters and order_by operate only on top-level keys (doc.get(field)). Firestore supports dotted field paths (a.b.c) for both queries and patch updates; ce-db Patch also only merges top-level fields, so nested objects are wholesale-replaced, not field-merged.

No FieldValue.serverTimestamp(), increment(), arrayUnion/arrayRemove. These are common Firestore write primitives; here every write is a literal value, so counters race and concurrent array edits clobber.

Collection state lives only in ce-coord's in-memory union plus the writer log; there is no local on-disk document cache for fast cold start independent of replaying the log, and no explicit offline persistence layer beyond what ce-coord provides. Compact is per-device only and does not GC tombstones across the merged set.
No way to enumerate collections, get doc create/update times, or read a document's version/metadata (Firestore exposes updateTime, createTime, readTime). Snapshot.op_count is the only coarse version.

robustnessIssues.issue: Capability gating is defined but never enforced on any data operation. Collection::set/patch/delete/get/query (src/collection.rs:110-158) perform reads/writes with zero authorization check; CollectionGrant::verify (src/access.rs:169) is only exercised by tests and the CLI grant/inspect commands. The 'security rules / IAM' row of the README table is aspirational at the call sites.

No bounds/limits on document size, field count, value size, op count, or number of followed peers. A single huge JSON document or an unbounded number of ops/writers will grow the in-memory union unboundedly (every read re-folds it). No validation that a doc_id or collection name is well-formed/length-bounded. This is a DoS / OOM surface with no backpressure.

Every read/get/query/len calls merged.read which calls pull() then a full O(n) fold of the entire op union (ce-coord merged.rs:253). There is no materialized/cached document map invalidated on change, so read cost grows linearly with total history (not live doc count). For a long-lived collection this degrades badly even after compaction.

Realtime is poll-driven, not push. watch() only fires when pull() runs, and pull() runs only inside read/propose/op_count. Peer ops arriving over the mesh do not wake a watcher until something calls refresh()/read(); the live test confirms this by looping with refresh() + 300ms sleep. next_change therefore cannot deliver peer changes without an external poller, contradicting the 'onSnapshot listeners' framing.

Lamport clock uses two separate lock acquisitions (advance_clock_past_merged then next_key both lock self.lamport) with a read of merged_ops between them, so concurrent writers on the same Collection can interleave and produce a non-strictly-increasing local sequence under contention; the (lamport,writer) key still won't collide across devices but same-device concurrency is not airtight.

lock().unwrap() on the Lamport mutex in production write paths will panic the task if the mutex is poisoned (e.g. a panic while holding it elsewhere). Standards forbid unwrap in prod paths; should be a lock-poison-tolerant access or parking_lot mutex.

compact() snapshots only THIS device's writer log; it does not compact peer logs or garbage-collect tombstones from the merged union, so deleted documents' tombstones and old patch ops persist forever in the folded set, and a fresh follower of a peer still replays that peer's full log.

Query order() with f64 total_cmp coerces all JSON numbers through as_f64 (query.rs:159), losing precision for i64/u64 values beyond 2^53 in comparisons/ordering even though the values round-trip exactly in storage. A large-integer field will sort/compare incorrectly.

CLI parse_filter swallows JSON parse errors and silently falls back to treating the value as a bare string (bin/ce_db.rs:245-246), so a typo like age:gt:3O becomes a string compare that silently returns nothing rather than erroring.

testGaps.area: Capability enforcement at the data layer (there is none, so there is nothing to test) — once a guarded Collection exists, needs read/write denial tests
Concurrent same-device writes hammering next_key/advance_clock to prove monotonic local Lamport allocation under contention
Document/field/op-count size limits and rejection of oversized inputs (no limits exist, so no tests)
Realtime peer-change delivery WITHOUT an external poll loop — current live test masks the poll dependency with refresh()+sleep; needs a test asserting watch wakes on a peer op
Compaction correctness: snapshot+tail == full replay at the ce-db level (only asserted that a checkpoint is produced, not that a bootstrapped reader matches a full-replay reader)
Multi-writer (>2) convergence and writer-churn (add_writer mid-stream) over the live mesh
Query ordering precision for integers beyond 2^53 (proptest currently bounds vals to 0..1000)
CLI argument parsing and error paths (no tests for bin/ce_db.rs build_query/parse_filter/grant/inspect)
Lock-poisoning behavior of the Lamport mutex

docGaps: No examples/ directory; the only runnable example is the doctest in lib.rs and the CLI. A worked end-to-end example (two collections, capability handoff, snapshot bootstrap) would help.

README and rustdoc claim 'security rules / IAM' and 'onSnapshot realtime listeners' as delivered, but capability checks are not enforced on data ops and realtime is poll-driven — the docs should be explicit that CollectionGrant is a verification toolkit not an enforcement layer, and that watch needs a poller.
No architecture/design doc covering the O(n)-fold-per-read cost model, unbounded log growth, and the CRDT-vs-consensus boundary beyond the brief README 'What ce-db is not' section.
No CHANGELOG, no documented limits (max doc size, max collection size, supported query shapes), no MSRV note.
Layout section of README lists only realtime_sync.rs under tests/ but four test files exist (edge_cases, prop_db, live, realtime_sync) — stale.
No doctest/example for compaction or for following peers via add_writer.

ce-cdn.name: ce-cdn
ce-cdn.currentState: ce-cdn is a genuinely working, well-architected Rust application (edition 2024, ~4900 LOC) that reimagines Cloud CDN over CE primitives. The design cleanly separates pure, clock-injected, exhaustively-tested cores (cidrange RFC-7233 range math, cache TTL+LRU+hit/miss accounting, edge response shaping) from the I/O shells (a real hyper 1.x HTTP front-end exposing GET /cdn/<cid> + /status + /health, and a mesh host loop over the cdn/* request/reply protocol). Authorization is real: public CIDs are open; private content requires BOTH a ce-cap capability chain AND an Ed25519 proof-of-possession (pop.rs) binding X-Ce-Node-Id to (requester, cid, ability, expires) — closing a leaked-bearer-token hole, with the regression covered by tests. Content-addressing is leveraged correctly: the CID is the cache key and the integrity proof (get_object/reassemble re-verify every chunk), making cache poisoning and staleness structurally impossible, so cache-control is `immutable` and entries only leave on TTL/eviction. Almost nothing is stubbed — there is one genuinely-guarded `unreachable!` and no todo!/unimplemented!. The main gaps are missing CDN features (compression, on-demand invalidation/purge propagation by tag, real signed-URL links, multi-range, conditional requests) and that the publisher-side re-replication maintenance loop and payment-channel rent are designed (catalog supports replica health, replication.rs computes need) but not wired into a running command.
plan: Add a size cap to the mesh read path: introduce a MAX_MESH_OBJECT_BYTES constant; have do_read/ReadResp refuse (with a typed reason) or chunk objects above the SDK wire limit, and make do_read manifest/range-aware so a range request fetches only covering chunks instead of the whole object (mirror client.rs get_range on the host).

Add conditional-request support: read If-None-Match in edge::serve and server.rs and answer 304 Not Modified when the ETag matches the requested CID — near-free given immutable CIDs and the single biggest bandwidth win.

Bound the HTTP origin-fetch memory: enforce a max-object-size before/while fetching, stream large bodies instead of buffering+cloning into the cache, and add a request/idle timeout and max-header-size on the hyper server to close slowloris.

Wire a real re-replication maintenance loop: a `ce-cdn maintain` command (or background task in serve) that, per catalog entry, probes recorded+DHT edges via cdn/status, updates EdgeReplica.healthy, and calls replicate_to on freshly-ranked edges until replicas_needed == 0; persist results atomically.
Make catalog persistence crash-safe: write to a temp file in the same dir and atomically rename over catalog.json, and take an advisory file lock so concurrent CLI invocations don't race.

Implement fan-out purge: on `ce-cdn purge`, discover ALL edges via find_service(cdn:<cid>) (not just catalog-recorded ones) and purge each; add prefix/tag invalidation by storing tags in the catalog and resolving them to CIDs.
Add negative caching: cache 404/origin-miss results with a short negative TTL in EdgeCache (a tombstone variant) so repeated misses don't hammer the origin; add stale-if-error pass-through.
Add compression: store and serve gzip/brotli variants as distinct CIDs, negotiate via Accept-Encoding, and emit Content-Encoding + Vary; record the variant mapping in the catalog/manifest.
Add PoP replay protection: keep a bounded seen-nonce set keyed by (requester, sig) with the same FIFO-ring pattern as SeenTokens, rejecting a proof signature reused within its TTL.

Implement presigned time-limited public links (query-param signature over cid+expires verifiable by the edge against a publisher key) so private content can be shared without the consumer holding a key — the Cloud CDN signed-URL analog.

Add a metrics surface: per-CID hit/miss/bytes counters and a Prometheus-format endpoint; optionally persist cache + counters to disk so an edge restart does not cold-start, and document operational guidance.

Add the missing test coverage: integration tests driving the mesh host loop with a fake CeClient, concurrent cold-miss single-flight, attenuated/revoked capability chains on both paths, catalog corruption recovery, and large-object bounds — then wire payment-channel rent (channel_open/sign_receipt/channel_close) for serving.

featureGaps.feature: Content compression (gzip/brotli) with Accept-Encoding negotiation and Vary
featureGaps.notes: Cloud CDN compresses text assets and serves per-encoding variants. ce-cdn never inspects Accept-Encoding, never emits Content-Encoding/Vary, and stores only raw bytes. For a content-addressed store this means a separate CID per encoding plus negotiation in edge::serve/server.rs — entirely absent.

featureGaps.feature: Cache invalidation / purge propagation across all edges (by CID, prefix, or tag)
featureGaps.notes: purge_at targets one edge at a time from the catalog's recorded replicas; there is no fan-out purge to ALL advertising edges (find_service(cdn:<cid>) is not consulted on purge), no wildcard/prefix/tag invalidation, and no async purge job. A CID advertised by an edge not in the local catalog is never purged.

replication.rs (needs_rereplication/replicas_needed/select) and catalog edge-health are built and unit-tested, but no `ce-cdn` command or background loop probes replicas and re-caches to restore the target replication factor. The desired-replication field is recorded and never enforced after the initial put.

A strong ETag ("<cid>") is emitted but the edge never reads If-None-Match, so it can never answer 304 Not Modified — it always ships the full body. This is core CDN bandwidth savings and trivial given immutable CIDs.

Cloud CDN signed URLs grant time-bounded access to otherwise-private content via a URL query param, no client key needed. ce-cdn's PoP requires the caller to hold a private key and mint a per-request signature — strong, but not a shareable link. A presigned query-param mode (like the ce-storage presign referenced in pop.rs) is missing.
Explicitly unsupported — multipart ranges silently degrade to full 200. Real CDNs serve them; video/PDF clients use them.
A 404/origin error is never cached, so a missing CID re-hits the origin on every request (origin DoS amplification). No stale-while-revalidate / stale-if-error, no 5xx pass-through distinct from 404.

featureGaps.feature: Cache-tier / sharding and geo/topology-aware edge selection
featureGaps.notes: replication.rs admits it approximates geo-spread by picking distinct high-reputation edges; there is no latency-graph/region signal feeding placement, so 'edge' proximity to the consumer is not actually optimized.

featureGaps.feature: Metrics export and per-CID hit/miss observability beyond /status
featureGaps.notes: /status returns aggregate counters + per-CID sizes, but there is no per-CID hit/miss breakdown, no bandwidth/egress accounting, no Prometheus/structured metrics endpoint, and counters are in-memory only (lost on restart).
EdgeCache is purely in-memory; an edge restart drops all cached bytes and all stats, forcing cold re-fetch of every object. Cloud CDN edges persist. A disk-backed tier is absent.
README and module docs promise edge rent via CE payment channels 'wired by the CLI incrementally'; no channel_open/sign_receipt/channel_close calls exist anywhere in src/. Serving is currently free.

robustnessIssues.issue: Mesh read reply hex-encodes the entire object into a JSON string (ReadResp.bytes_hex), doubling memory and with no upper size bound — a large CID over cdn/read can OOM the edge and the requester; the SDK request/reply also has a wire-size ceiling that a big object will silently exceed/truncate.

No maximum-object-size limit on the HTTP origin-fetch path: server fetches the full object into memory then clones it to insert into the cache (b.clone()), so concurrent cold misses of large objects multiply memory. EdgeCache.insert rejects objects larger than the whole budget but only AFTER the full bytes are already in RAM.

Proof-of-possession has no replay/nonce cache: a captured X-Ce-Proof is replayable for the same (cid, ability) for up to MAX_PROOF_TTL_SECS (300s). Bound to the request, so limited, but a leaked proof+chain over a logging proxy is reusable within the window.

Catalog save is non-atomic: serde_json::to_vec_pretty then std::fs::write directly over catalog.json. A crash mid-write or concurrent `ce-cdn` invocations corrupt/truncate the index (no temp-file + rename, no file lock).
Negative results are not cached, so a flood of GETs for a public-but-absent CID hits the origin on every request (no negative TTL) — origin-amplification DoS.

EdgeCache.drop_entry does `self.cur_bytes -= e.bytes.len()` without saturating/checked subtraction; an invariant break (e.g. future code path inserting without updating cur_bytes) would panic on underflow in a release-debug build. Currently safe by construction but fragile.

do_read on a cold mesh read fetches the whole object even for a tiny range request (no manifest-aware partial fetch on the host side), so a 1-byte range over a 1 GiB object pulls and caches the entire object. The client-side get_range is chunk-aware; the host serve path is not.

requester_id defaults a malformed/absent X-Ce-Node-Id to all-zeros [0u8;32]; relies on no real key being all-zero and on PoP+cap both failing. Correct today, but a silent all-zero identity is a footgun if any code path ever treats zero as a valid root.
No HTTP request timeout / slowloris protection on the hyper accept loop; serve_connection runs unbounded per connection and there is no read/idle timeout or max-header-size override.

Path parsing for /cdn/<cid> does not URL-decode or validate CID charset/length; a percent-encoded or overlong segment is passed straight to the origin/cache as a key (cache-key confusion, though content-addressing limits the blast radius).

testGaps.area: Mesh host loop end-to-end (cdn/cache, cdn/read whole+range, cdn/purge, cdn/status over request/reply)
cache_object / read_object against a fake CeClient/origin (cold-miss fetch+cache, budget-exceeded bail, error propagation)
Concurrent HTTP reads of the same cold CID (single-flight / thundering-herd: origin should be hit once, not N times under contention)
SeenTokens dedup under concurrent loop iterations and the revocation-refresh path (revoked set wired into authorize)
PoP replay within the TTL window and clock-skew boundary (expires == now, expires == now+MAX_TTL)
Catalog corruption / partial-write recovery and concurrent writer races
client.rs put/get/get_range/pick_edges/replicate_to/probe/purge_at — these public methods have only struct-shape unit tests, no behavioral coverage with a mock CeClient
Large-object bounds: oversize mesh ReadResp / oversize HTTP object memory behavior
Multi-level / attenuated capability chains and revoked capability rejection on both HTTP and mesh paths (current tests use single self-issued chains)
HEAD request range handling (HEAD + Range -> 206 headers, no body) and method/path edge cases (trailing slash, empty cid)

docGaps: No rustdoc doctests (examples in /// comments are prose, none are ```compile-tested```); the single runnable example requires a live CE node and silently returns Ok if absent.
No architecture/design doc (docs/ directory absent) explaining the CE-vs-app boundary, the trust model, or how this differs from ce-pin/ce-storage beyond inline module headers.
README claims '72 unit + 17 integration tests' as a fixed count — brittle and unverifiable in CI; no coverage report.

Payment-channel rent and the re-replication loop are documented as features in README but not implemented — README overstates current capability (says replication happens but the maintenance/health loop that keeps replicas alive does not exist).
No CHANGELOG, no documented HTTP error-body format, and the /status JSON schema is only described by example, not specified.

No operational docs: how to run an edge in production, recommended --max-mb, security posture of exposing the HTTP front-end publicly, or how X-Ce-Proof is minted by a real client (the client never sets it).

ce-pin.name: ce-pin
ce-pin.currentState: ce-pin is a Rust (edition 2024) CLI + library app on the CE SDK (ce-rs) implementing IPFS-style content pinning over the mesh. Architecture is clean and well-factored: pure modules (audit PoR, placement ranking, proto wire types, pinset index) are network-free and genuinely well unit/property-tested, while client.rs/host.rs wrap ce-rs and the pin/* mesh protocol. What genuinely works: chunked publish via put_object (CID = manifest hash, integrity is the proof), fetch-by-CID, DHT announce/discover via advertise_service/find_service, capability-gated (ce-cap) host serve() loop honoring pin:store/read/audit/release with attenuation/expiry/revocation, ranked replication to N peers, and beacon-seeded proof-of-retrievability. A real 2-node live_mesh test drives the actual host loop end-to-end (skips cleanly if no ce binary). What is stubbed or absent: payment/rent is entirely unwired — Replica.channel_id is always the empty string and channel_open/sign_receipt/channel_close (which exist in ce-rs) are never called, so "paid pinning" is terms-only; there is no GC/eviction, no host-side size/capacity admission control, no expiry enforcement, and no auto re-replication daemon. The PoR audit has a self-acknowledged residual hole (H3): get_blob falls back to mesh re-fetch so byte-level proofs can be forged by a host that discarded bytes; only the held-set name gate mitigates it today.
plan: Add host-side admission control in do_offer: reject offers above a configurable max-object-size, below a configurable min rent_per_gb_hour, and when projected disk usage would exceed a capacity budget; return structured OfferResp{accepted:false,reason}. This closes the highest-severity DoS and makes pricing real.

Implement capacity accounting + GC/eviction on the host: track bytes held per CID and total, persist alongside the held-set, and evict by (expired > lowest-rent > LRU) when over the capacity budget. Add `ce-pin host-gc` and surface held/total bytes.

Wire payment channels for rent: in cmd_add, channel_open per accepted replica (store the real channel_id, not String::new()), run a periodic sign_receipt loop crediting GB-hours, and have the host redeem via channel_close on release/expiry. This is the feature the app is named around and is currently absent.

Build the auto re-replication / repair daemon (`ce-pin watch`): periodically audit_replica each pin, and when healthy_replicas() < desired, call replicate(exclude=current healthy holders) to restore the factor, persisting updated replicas. The building blocks already exist.

Make serve() robust for long uptime: bound/evict the `seen` token set (e.g. time- or size-windowed), process offers concurrently (spawn per-request tasks or a bounded worker pool) to remove head-of-line blocking, and add a timeout around get_object.

Make held-set and pinset writes atomic (write-tmp+rename) and stop silently dropping commitments on a corrupt held.json — on parse failure, back up and warn rather than unwrap_or_default to default().
Enforce expiry: have hosts drop (and stop advertising) pins past expiry_height, and add a publisher-side `ce-pin renew <cid>` that extends expiry and tops up the channel.
Add geo/fault-domain diversity to placement: incorporate region/ASN/latency signals (from atlas/latency graph) so the N replicas are spread across fault domains, not co-located.
Implement true unpin: add a CLI `ce-pin release <cid>` that sends pin/release to current holders (and closes their channels), and have cmd_rm optionally trigger it instead of only forgetting locally.
Fix status health write-back to be per-replica (use each replica's own probe/audit result, not the global `live>0` flag) so persisted last_proof_ok is accurate.

Coordinate with the node team on the H3 local-only blob read (GET /blobs/:hash?local=1 → 404 on local miss, surfaced as CeClient::get_blob_local) and switch do_audit to it, then add an e2e test proving a host that deleted bytes fails byte-level PoR.
Add directory/recursive pinning, async pin-job status states (queued/pinning/pinned/failed), streaming reads for large files, doctests on pure functions, and host metrics counters.

featureGaps.notes: channel_open/sign_receipt/channel_close exist in ce-rs but are never called. Replica.channel_id is hardcoded to String::new() in client.rs:128. add never opens a channel; serve never tracks accrued GB-hours or redeems receipts. 'Paid pinning' is currently just a rent_per_gb_hour string with no economic enforcement — a host gets nothing and a publisher pays nothing.

host.rs comments admit 'The MVP does not garbage-collect blobs; a real host would.' No disk-usage accounting, no high-water-mark eviction, no LRU/rent-priority eviction, no per-host capacity budget. do_offer (host.rs:168) accepts any object regardless of size or free space.

featureGaps.feature: Host-side admission control / size & rate limits
featureGaps.notes: do_offer ignores req.bytes_len entirely and calls get_object unconditionally. No max-object-size, no min-acceptable-rent check (despite the proto comment 'host decides if the rate is worth it'), no per-publisher quota, no concurrency cap. An authorized publisher can force unbounded fetches and unbounded held-set growth (DoS).

featureGaps.feature: Automatic repair / re-pin to maintain replication factor
featureGaps.notes: README admits the long-running daemon that ties audit_replica + replicate(exclude=current holders) together is 'the next step'. status --audit measures health but never triggers re-replication. healthy_replicas() is computed but nothing acts on a shortfall. No background loop.
PinJob.expiry_height and OfferReq.expiry_height are recorded but never enforced. A host never drops a pin past expiry; a publisher never renews. No lease-renewal command.

placement.rs ranks only by delivered_work, last_seen_secs, mem_mb. No region/ASN/latency diversity, so all N replicas can land in one datacenter or on correlated peers — the opposite of a real pinning service's geo-distribution guarantee.

Real pinning APIs expose queued/pinning/pinned/failed per replica. Here a pin is binary accepted/declined; offer blocks up to 120s on a synchronous fetch with no async pin-job/progress, and no requested-vs-actual replication reconciliation surfaced beyond ls counts.

featureGaps.feature: Bulk / recursive / directory pinning and unpin-from-host
featureGaps.notes: add takes a single file only; no directory/recursive add, no manifest-of-manifests, no batch. rm only forgets locally — there is a pin/release protocol message and host handler but no CLI command to actually tell hosts to release (orphaned rent/garbage on hosts).

featureGaps.feature: Private/encrypted content (pin:read gating actually serving bytes)
featureGaps.notes: pin:read ability and 'cap-gated privacy' are advertised, but content is in the shared content-addressed blob store and get_object is open; there is no encryption-at-rest or read-gated byte serving path. Privacy claim is aspirational.
No counters for held bytes, accepted/declined offers, audit pass rate, or rent earned; only ad-hoc tracing. A real storage edge exposes capacity and serving metrics.

robustnessIssues.issue: Unbounded memory growth in serve() loop: the `seen: HashSet<u64>` of reply tokens (host.rs:44) and `revoked` set grow without bound for the life of a long-running host; tokens are never evicted, so a busy/long-lived host leaks memory monotonically.

No size/capacity admission control: do_offer unconditionally calls get_object on any CID an authorized caller names, ignoring req.bytes_len; combined with unbounded held-set this is a disk/bandwidth DoS from any holder of a pin:store cap.

Minimum-rent / economic check absent: the proto documents 'the host decides if the rate is worth it' but do_offer never inspects rent_per_gb_hour, so hosts accept zero-rent pins. Pricing is purely decorative.

PoR byte-level forgery (self-acknowledged H3): do_audit gates on the held-set name only, then reads chunk bytes via get_blob which falls back to mesh fetch-by-hash; a host that committed-then-deleted bytes can re-pull the challenged chunk from a sibling replica and forge a passing proof. The economic availability guarantee is not enforced until the node adds a local-only blob read (get_blob_local).

HeldSet persisted as plain JSON written non-atomically (std::fs::write directly over the file); a crash mid-write corrupts held.json and HeldSet::load silently falls back to default() via unwrap_or_default (host.rs:278), silently dropping ALL pin commitments on a corrupt file.

Concurrency hazard: serve() holds a single in-process HeldSet mutated per message with no locking; fine single-task, but pin/release and pin/offer both save() the file — if two host processes share CE_PIN_DIR (e.g. operator runs two) there is a last-writer-wins lost-update on held.json with no file lock.

status command writes back replica health with weak logic: last_proof_ok is set to `all.contains(holder) && live>0 && holders.contains(holder)` (main.rs:271) — a global `live>0` flag marks a replica healthy even if that specific replica failed its own probe, so persisted health can be wrong.
No bound on object/file size in add: std::fs::read(file) loads the entire file into memory (main.rs:128) with no streaming and no max-size guard; a multi-GB file OOMs the client.

audit_replica returns Ok(true) for an empty-chunks manifest (client.rs:146) — 'empty object trivially retrievable' — meaning a host that holds nothing for a zero-chunk CID always passes, and a malicious manifest with chunks=[] would auto-pass.
reply_token collision/replay: `seen.insert(token)` dedups by u64 token only; if the node ever reuses a token value (wraparound or restart) a legitimate later request is silently dropped.

No timeout/cancellation around get_object in do_offer — a slow or stalled mesh fetch can hang the single serve() task for the full SDK timeout, blocking ALL other pin requests (head-of-line blocking) since messages are processed sequentially in one loop.

testGaps.area: Host serve() loop: admission, held-set persistence across restart, release path, dedup/seen behavior
Payment-channel rent flow (currently nonexistent) — once wired, open/receipt/redeem
Auto re-replication on failed audit (repair loop) — no test because feature absent
Concurrent offers / head-of-line blocking and parallel pin handling under load
HeldSet corruption recovery (truncated/partial JSON silently dropping commitments)
do_offer with oversized object / no-capacity host (admission limits)
status health write-back correctness (per-replica vs global live flag)
Mesh fetch-failure paths in do_offer/do_audit (host returns structured negative replies)
H3 byte-level PoR forgery via mesh re-fetch — live_mesh tests the name-gate but cannot prove the byte-level guarantee until node supports local-only reads

docGaps: README 'Money model' and 'Status' sections describe payment-channel rent settlement as the design but it is entirely unimplemented; the doc soft-pedals this ('the first follow-up') rather than clearly stating no payment occurs today.
No rustdoc/architecture note on capacity/GC strategy — host.rs only has an inline 'a real host would' aside; no doc on how a real operator bounds disk.
No doctests on any public function (audit::prove/verify, placement::rank, proto::service_for are obvious candidates and would double as examples).
No CHANGELOG, no versioned API stability note for the pin/* wire protocol (proto.rs) despite it being a cross-node contract.
examples/ contains only demo.sh/demo.ps1; no documented example of issuing the ce-cap chain end-to-end or of the (missing) rent loop.
The H3 residual-trust limitation is well documented in host.rs but the README PoR section understates it ('raises the cost of lying') without naming the concrete mesh-refetch forgery vector.

ce-drive.name: ce-drive
